studyMetadata.title: Introducing Mark
studyMetadata.subtitle: Bible Study
studyMetadata.description:
Many people are curious about Jesus. Who was He really? Why did He attract such attention? What made His life so different from anyone else’s? Mark doesn’t begin with background or explanation. It moves straight into the action and lets you see Jesus for yourself.
‘The beginning of the good news about Jesus the Messiah, the Son of God.’ (Mark 1:1)
From the outset, Mark makes a bold claim. Jesus is not just a teacher or prophet. He is the Messiah – God’s chosen King – and the Son of God. Everything that follows invites you to watch Him, listen to Him, and decide what you will do with Him.

elements.question: It’s worth reading the whole passage before you begin...
elements.answer:
1 The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, the Son of God. 2 As it is written in the prophet Isaiah,
“Look, I am sending my messenger ahead of you,
who will prepare your way,
3 the voice of one shouting in the wilderness,
‘Prepare the way for the Lord,
make his paths straight.’”
4 In the wilderness John the baptizer began preaching a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins. 5 People from the whole Judean countryside and all of Jerusalem were going out to him, and he was baptizing them in the Jordan River as they confessed their sins. 6 John wore a garment made of camel’s hair with a leather belt around his waist, and he ate locusts and wild honey. 7 He proclaimed, “One more powerful than I am is coming after me; I am not worthy to bend down and untie the strap of his sandals. 8 I baptize you with water, but he will baptize you with the Holy Spirit.”
9 Now in those days Jesus came from Nazareth in Galilee and was baptized by John in the Jordan River. 10 And just as Jesus was coming up out of the water, he saw the heavens splitting apart and the Spirit descending on him like a dove. 11 And a voice came from heaven: “You are my one dear Son; in you I take great delight.” 12 The Spirit immediately drove him into the wilderness. 13 He was in the wilderness forty days, enduring temptations from Satan. He was with wild animals, and angels were ministering to his needs.
14 Now after John was imprisoned, Jesus went into Galilee and proclaimed the gospel of God. 15 He said, “The time is fulfilled and the kingdom of God is near. Repent and believe the gospel!”

elements.text:
Mark wastes no time. There is no birth narrative, no lengthy introduction. From the very first sentence, he tells us exactly who Jesus is — ”The beginning of the good news about Jesus the Messiah, the Son of God.” That is the headline. Everything else in the Gospel is the story beneath it.

But as you read on, something surprising emerges. People hear about Jesus. They see what he does. And yet they don’t really understand him. Crowds are amazed. Disciples are confused. Religious leaders are threatened. Even those closest to Jesus misread him again and again. Which raises a question that runs right through Mark’s Gospel — and that these studies will keep bringing us back to: Who is Jesus — and are we seeing him clearly?
The Beginning of Good News

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, the Son of God. <sup>2</sup>As it is written in Isaiah the prophet, “Look, I am sending my messenger ahead of you, who will prepare your way, <sup>3</sup>the voice of one shouting in the wilderness, ‘Prepare the way for the Lord, make his paths straight.’”

elements.question: What does Mark want us to understand about Jesus from the very first verse?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Mark identifies Jesus with two titles in v1: “Jesus Christ” (meaning Jesus the Messiah, God’s long-awaited chosen King) and ”the Son of God.” These are not throwaway descriptions — they are Mark’s headline for the whole Gospel. He then immediately reaches back into the Old Testament to show that Jesus’s arrival was foretold by the prophet Isaiah: a messenger would prepare the way for the Lord himself. By quoting this prophecy, Mark is saying that in Jesus, God’s ancient promises are being fulfilled. Jesus is not just another religious figure; he is the one Israel had been waiting for, and his coming is the definitive act of God in history.
elements.questionHint: Look carefully at the two titles Mark uses in v1 — what do each of them claim about Jesus, and why might it matter that he puts them in the very first sentence?

elements.text: The phrase ”good news” in Mark’s world was not a religious phrase — it was a royal one. When a king won a great victory, or when an heir to the throne was born, heralds would go out with the ”good news.” It was a public announcement of something decisive that had happened. Mark is saying: God is acting. The King is coming. This is not advice for living a better life — it is an announcement that changes everything.

elements.question: Why is this described as ”good news”? What expectations might people have had about God’s coming King?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The Old Testament passage Mark quotes in v2–3 is from Isaiah, a prophet who repeatedly promised that God would one day return to his people, defeat their enemies, forgive their sins, and restore what had been lost. For a people who had suffered centuries of foreign domination and longed for God to act, the arrival of the one Isaiah described would be extraordinarily good news. It would mean that the waiting was over, that God had kept his promises, and that the age of rescue and restoration had finally arrived. ”Good news” here is not about personal self-improvement — it is the announcement that the King who can set everything right has come.
elements.questionHint: The prophecy Mark quotes in v2–3 uses the language of ”preparing the way for the Lord” — who in the Old Testament context was this about, and what does it mean that Mark applies it to the arrival of Jesus?

elements.text: Preparing the Way

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>In the wilderness John the baptizer began preaching a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins. <sup>5</sup>People from the whole Judean countryside and all of Jerusalem were going out to him, and he was baptizing them in the Jordan River as they confessed their sins. <sup>6</sup>John wore a garment made of camel’s hair with a leather belt around his waist, and he ate locusts and wild honey. <sup>7</sup>He proclaimed, “One more powerful than I am is coming after me; I am not worthy to bend down and untie the strap of his sandals. <sup>8</sup>I baptize you with water, but he will baptize you with the Holy Spirit.”

elements.question: Why is repentance the right preparation for meeting Jesus?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: John preaches a ”baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins” (v4). The logic is clear: if Jesus is the holy and powerful one who is coming — one so great that John declares himself unworthy even to untie his sandals (v7) — then the right way to meet him is not with self-confidence but with honest acknowledgement of your need. Repentance means recognising that you have gone your own way rather than God’s, and turning around. It makes no sense to welcome a King who comes to forgive and restore unless you first acknowledge that you need forgiveness and restoration. John’s baptism prepared people by bringing them to the point of honest admission before the one who could actually deal with their sin.
elements.questionHint: Notice what John’s baptism was specifically ”for” in v4 — and then look at what he says about the one coming after him in v7–8. Why would you need to be prepared in that particular way before meeting someone like that?

elements.text: When a king was about to visit a town, people would prepare — roads were repaired, houses were tidied, officials were put in place. But when John the Baptist prepares the way for Jesus, he doesn’t call people to tidy their towns. He calls them to repent. You don’t prepare for this King by getting your outward life in order. You prepare by honestly facing the inward mess. Repentance is not gloomy — it is the realistic first step toward something wonderful.

elements.question: What does repentance actually involve? Why might people resist this message?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: In v4–5, John calls people to repentance and they come, confessing their sins. Repentance involves an honest admission of having sinned — not a general sense of being imperfect, but an actual confession of specific wrongdoing. The crowds confessing their sins in v5 suggests a real reckoning, not a superficial ritual. People might resist this message for many reasons: it requires admitting fault and giving up the pretence of self-sufficiency; it is humbling; and it demands change rather than just a change of scenery. Religious people in particular might resist it because they feel they are already doing well enough. The large crowds who do come, however, suggest that many recognised their need and found the message compelling rather than merely threatening.
elements.questionHint: What does the specific word ”confessing” in v5 tell us about how public and personal the response to John’s message was — and what might that demand cost someone?

elements.text: The Identity of Jesus Revealed

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>Now in those days Jesus came from Nazareth in Galilee and was baptized by John in the Jordan River. <sup>10</sup>And just as Jesus was coming up out of the water, he saw the heavens splitting apart and the Spirit descending on him like a dove. <sup>11</sup>And a voice came from heaven: “You are my one dear Son; in you I take great delight.”

elements.question: What do we learn about Jesus’ identity at his baptism?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Three things happen at the baptism, and together they reveal who Jesus is. The heavens tear open — a dramatic signal that God is breaking into this moment. The Spirit descends on Jesus like a dove — marking him out as the one uniquely anointed and empowered by God for his mission. And a voice from heaven declares: “You are my one dear Son; in you I take great delight.” This voice — the voice of God the Father — announces Jesus’s identity in the most direct possible terms. He is the beloved Son. Not a prophet, not a teacher, not a reformer — but God’s own Son, the one in whom the Father is fully and personally delighted. All three persons of the Trinity are present at this moment: Father, Son, and Spirit.
elements.questionHint: How many distinct things happen in v10–11, and who speaks or acts in each case — what picture of Jesus does that combination paint?

elements.text: Before Jesus has preached a single sermon or performed a single miracle, God speaks. ”You are my Son, whom I love; with you I am well pleased.” This is striking. Usually we think of identity as something we earn — through achievement, through reputation, through what others think of us. But here, Jesus’ identity is declared before anything he has done publicly. He is the beloved Son. That is the foundation, not the conclusion.

elements.question: Why is this declaration important at the very start of his ministry? How is this different from how we usually think about identity?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The declaration comes before any public ministry — before miracles, before preaching, before the cross. This matters enormously: Jesus’s identity as the beloved Son is not something he earns by performing well or establishing a reputation. It is spoken over him at the very outset, grounding everything that follows. In human life we typically build identity through achievement — we feel we have to prove our worth before we can be confident of who we are. Here, the opposite is true: identity precedes performance. The Father’s delight in the Son is declared first, and the ministry flows from that secure foundation rather than toward it. For those who are in Christ, this pattern matters: our identity as God’s children is likewise declared, not earned.
elements.questionHint: At what point in Jesus’s ministry does this declaration come — and what does its timing tell you about the relationship between identity and what we achieve?

elements.text: Tested but Faithful

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>The Spirit immediately drove him into the wilderness. <sup>13</sup>He was in the wilderness forty days, enduring temptations from Satan. He was with wild animals, and angels were ministering to his needs.

elements.question: Why is it important that Jesus faces temptation?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Mark is strikingly brief here compared to Matthew and Luke, but what he includes is telling. Jesus is tested by Satan for forty days — a number that echoes Israel’s forty years in the wilderness, where they repeatedly failed God’s tests. Jesus, unlike Israel, endures. The detail that he was ”with wild animals” may evoke the chaos and danger of the fallen world, and the angels ministering to him suggest that he is not abandoned but sustained through his trial. The importance of this is that Jesus enters his ministry having been genuinely tested and having stood firm. He is not a saviour who has bypassed human struggle — he has faced the enemy’s opposition and not been defeated. This is the one we are asked to trust and follow.
elements.questionHint: The number forty has a particular significance in the Old Testament story of Israel — what happened to Israel in their forty years of testing, and why might it matter that Jesus’s forty days ends differently?

elements.text: Immediately after his baptism, Jesus is driven into the desert. He is tested. Mark doesn’t give us the details that Matthew and Luke do, but the point is clear: where human beings have failed again and again — where Israel failed, where Adam failed — Jesus stands firm. This matters enormously. His faithfulness is not a footnote; it is part of what makes him the Saviour we need.

elements.question: What does this show about the mission Jesus has come to fulfil?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The sequence in Mark is deliberate: baptism (identity declared), then immediately the wilderness (identity tested). Jesus is not gently eased into ministry — the Spirit ”drives” him into confrontation with Satan. This tells us something important about his mission: it involves direct combat with the powers of evil from the very start. He has not come merely to teach wisdom or model a good life; he has come to engage and overcome the enemy of human souls. His survival of the temptation is the first skirmish in a war that will culminate at the cross. The mission he has come to fulfil requires precisely this kind of faithful endurance under pressure.
elements.questionHint: Notice the word Mark uses in v12 for how Jesus got to the wilderness — it was not a gentle stroll. What does that urgency suggest about the nature of the mission Jesus is undertaking?

elements.text: The Message of the Kingdom

elements.passageText: <sup>14</sup>Now after John was imprisoned, Jesus went into Galilee and proclaimed the gospel of God. <sup>15</sup>He said, “The time is fulfilled and the kingdom of God is near. Repent and believe the gospel!”

elements.question: What does it mean that ”the kingdom of God has come near”?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus says two things in close succession in v15: “the time is fulfilled” and ”the kingdom of God is near.” Together they mean that the long-awaited moment has arrived. The ”time” refers to the period of waiting and preparation — the whole sweep of Old Testament history pointing forward. That time is now complete. And the kingdom of God — God’s own sovereign rule and reign breaking into the world — has drawn near in the person of Jesus himself. The kingdom is not primarily a place or a future arrangement; it is the active reign of God made present. Where Jesus is, the kingdom is. His healings, his authority over evil, his forgiveness of sins — all of these are signs that God’s reign is arriving and displacing the disorder of the old order.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says two things in v15: “the time is fulfilled” and ”the kingdom of God is near” — what is the relationship between those two statements, and what does each one add?

elements.text: ”Repent and believe the good news!” That is Jesus’ summary of his own message. It has two parts, and they belong together. Repentance without faith is just guilt. Faith without repentance is just wishful thinking. Together, they describe a complete turning — away from one way of living, and toward the King who has arrived. The kingdom of God is not a distant hope. It has come near. In Jesus, it has arrived.

elements.question: Why do repentance and faith go together? How should people respond to this message?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus’s command in v15 has two parts: “repent” and ”believe the gospel.” They are not alternatives — both are required. Repentance means turning away: acknowledging that the direction you have been heading is wrong and turning around. Belief means turning toward: trusting that the good news about Jesus is true and staking your life on it. Neither is complete without the other. Repentance without faith would leave you only with guilt and regret, with nowhere to turn. Faith without repentance would leave you claiming to trust Jesus while continuing in the same direction as before. Together they describe the appropriate whole-life response to the news that the King has arrived — a decisive reorientation of how you live and who you live for.
elements.questionHint: Look at the structure of Jesus’s command in v15 — it has two parts. What would be lost if you tried to do only one of them without the other?

elements.text: Reflection Questions

elements.question: What assumptions do you bring about who Jesus is? Where have those assumptions come from?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: We often reshape Jesus to fit our expectations rather than letting him reshape us. Can you think of an area where you might be doing that?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: If repentance and faith go together at the start of the Christian life, how are you doing at living out both of them today?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: What would it mean for you — practically and specifically — to respond to Jesus as King this week?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.text: Prayer Focus

Ask God to help you see Jesus clearly, not through the filter of your expectations, but as Mark presents him. Confess any areas where you have been resisting what he calls you to. Ask for the faith to trust him, and the courage to follow.
Application Challenge
Each morning this week, begin the day with this short prayer: “Jesus, you are King — help me to follow you today.” Notice what it brings up. Notice where it feels costly.

elements.text:
There is a difference between being impressed by someone and being willing to submit to them. You might be impressed by a surgeon’s skill without trusting her with your own operation. You might admire a great leader without following his instructions. Amazement and submission are not the same thing.

In this section of Mark, people are constantly amazed by Jesus. But amazement is not the same as understanding — and understanding is not the same as obedience. As we look at Jesus’ authority in teaching, in action, and over sin itself, the question to keep asking is not ”Is Jesus impressive?” but ”Am I submitted to him?”
The Call to Follow

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>As he went along the Sea of Galilee, he saw Simon and Andrew, Simon’s brother, casting a net into the sea (for they were fishermen). <sup>17</sup>Jesus said to them, “Follow me, and I will turn you into fishers of people.” <sup>18</sup>They left their nets immediately and followed him. <sup>19</sup>Going on a little farther, he saw James, the son of Zebedee, and John his brother in their boat mending nets. <sup>20</sup>Immediately he called them, and they left their father Zebedee in the boat with the hired men and followed him.

elements.question: What stands out about how the disciples respond to Jesus’ call?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The most striking feature is the immediacy. Mark uses the word ”immediately” twice — “they left their nets immediately” (v18) and ”immediately he called them” (v20). There is no deliberation, no asking for time to think, no negotiating about conditions. Simon and Andrew leave their nets; James and John leave their father and the hired men mid-task. These are real costs — nets were a livelihood, and leaving your father in a boat was not trivial. And yet the response is instant. Mark seems to want us to feel the sheer weight of Jesus’s call: it demands everything, and the right response is to go, without delay and without conditions.
elements.questionHint: How many times does Mark use the word ”immediately” or ”at once” in describing this scene — and what does that repetition tell you about the kind of response Jesus’s call demands?

elements.text: Jesus says two words: “Follow me.” And they follow. Immediately. They leave nets, boats, and — in the case of James and John — their father. No negotiation, no questions, no conditions. This is not presented as reckless impulsiveness. It is presented as the right response to Jesus. His call is immediate and costly, and the right answer is to go.

elements.question: Why is their response so immediate? What might hold someone back from responding like that today?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The passage doesn’t explain why the response is so immediate — Mark seems to want us to feel the authority in Jesus’s call rather than provide a psychological explanation. The call itself carries such weight that delay would be the wrong response. What Jesus offers in v17 is also striking: not just a job but a new identity and purpose — “I will make you fishers of people.” He is not merely asking for their time; he is offering to reshape who they are. Today, what might hold someone back is exactly what these men left behind: security (nets and a livelihood), family obligations (a father in the boat), and the sense that you can’t just walk away from your responsibilities. The passage challenges us to ask whether any of those things have become more decisive than Jesus’s call.
elements.questionHint: Look at what the disciples physically leave behind in v18 and v20 — what categories of life do those things represent, and why might each one be particularly hard to walk away from?

elements.text: Authority in Teaching and Power

elements.passageText: <sup>21</sup>Then they went to Capernaum. When the Sabbath came, Jesus went into the synagogue and began to teach. <sup>22</sup>The people there were amazed by his teaching, because he taught them like one who had authority, not like the experts in the law. <sup>23</sup>Just then there was a man in their synagogue with an unclean spirit, and he cried out, <sup>24</sup>”Leave us alone, Jesus the Nazarene! Have you come to destroy us? I know who you are — the Holy One of God!” <sup>25</sup>But Jesus rebuked him: “Silence! Come out of him!” <sup>26</sup>After throwing him into convulsions, the unclean spirit cried out with a loud voice and came out of him. <sup>27</sup>They were all amazed so that they asked each other, “What is this? A new teaching with authority! He even commands the unclean spirits and they obey him.” <sup>28</sup>So the news about him spread quickly throughout all the region around Galilee. <sup>29</sup>Now as soon as they left the synagogue, they entered Simon and Andrew’s house, with James and John. <sup>30</sup>Simon’s mother-in-law was lying down, sick with a fever, so they spoke to Jesus at once about her. <sup>31</sup>He came and raised her up by gently taking her hand. Then the fever left her and she began to serve them. <sup>32</sup>When it was evening, after sunset, they brought to him all who were sick and demon-possessed. <sup>33</sup>The whole town gathered by the door. <sup>34</sup>So he healed many who were sick with various diseases and drove out many demons. But he would not permit the demons to speak, because they knew him.

elements.question: How do people react to Jesus’ teaching and actions?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The crowd in the synagogue is amazed by Jesus’s teaching (v22), and after the exorcism their amazement deepens further as they ask each other what this new teaching ”with authority” could be (v27). The news about him spreads quickly across the whole region (v28). When he heals Simon’s mother-in-law, the response is immediate service — she gets up and begins to wait on them (v31). By evening, the whole town has gathered at the door, bringing the sick and demon-possessed (v32–33). The response is a combination of wonder, curiosity, and urgent need — people are drawn to Jesus because what he does is unlike anything they have seen, even if they don’t yet understand who he really is.
elements.questionHint: Notice that the crowd uses the same word to describe both Jesus’s teaching and his power over the unclean spirit — what is that word, and why might Mark want us to see those two things as connected?

elements.text: Jesus teaches in the synagogue, and the crowd is astonished — not at the content, but at the authority. He doesn’t say, “Rabbi So-and-so argues that…” He simply speaks, and what he says carries weight. Then he acts: he commands an unclean spirit and it obeys. He heals the sick. Word spreads across the whole region. His authority is not limited to the lecture hall — it extends over the spiritual realm and the physical body. It is absolute and it is real.

elements.question: What makes his authority different from other teachers and leaders? Why might this be both attractive and unsettling?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The contrast in v22 is explicit: Jesus teaches ”not like the experts in the law.” The scribes derived their authority from tradition and scholarship — they quoted sources and precedents. Jesus speaks in his own name, with a direct, first-person weight that belongs to no one else in the room. His authority is also not theoretical: it is immediately demonstrated in action. The unclean spirit obeys him (v25–26); sickness departs at his touch (v31); even the demons recognise him (v24, 34). This is both attractive — here is someone who can actually do something about suffering and evil — and unsettling, because absolute authority demands an absolute response. You can’t admire it from a safe distance; you have to decide what to do with it.
elements.questionHint: Compare how Jesus speaks in v17 and v25 with how you might expect a teacher to speak — what is the grammatical difference, and what does it imply about where his authority comes from?

elements.text: Authority to Forgive

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Now after some days, when he returned to Capernaum, the news spread that he was at home. <sup>2</sup>So many gathered that there was no longer any room, not even by the door, and he preached the word to them. <sup>3</sup>Some people came bringing to him a paralytic, carried by four of them. <sup>4</sup>When they were not able to bring him in because of the crowd, they removed the roof above Jesus. Then, after tearing it out, they lowered the stretcher the paralytic was lying on. <sup>5</sup>When Jesus saw their faith, he said to the paralytic, “Son, your sins are forgiven.” <sup>6</sup>Now some of the experts in the law were sitting there, turning these things over in their minds: <sup>7”Why does this man speak this way? He is blaspheming! Who can forgive sins but God alone?” <sup>8</sup>Now immediately, when Jesus realized in his spirit that they were contemplating such thoughts, he said to them, “Why are you thinking such things in your hearts? <sup>9</sup>Which is easier, to say to the paralytic, ‘Your sins are forgiven,‘ or to say, ‘Stand up, take your stretcher, and walk’? <sup>10</sup>But so that you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins,“ — he said to the paralytic — <sup>11”I tell you, stand up, take your stretcher, and go home.” <sup>12</sup>And immediately the man stood up, took his stretcher, and went out in front of them all. They were all amazed and glorified God, saying, “We have never seen anything like this!”

elements.question: Why do the teachers of the law react so strongly to Jesus’ words?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Their objection in v7 is theologically precise and internally coherent: “Who can forgive sins but God alone?” They are right. In the Old Testament framework, forgiveness of sins was God’s prerogative — something that required a sacrifice and divine action, not a human declaration. When Jesus says ”your sins are forgiven,“ he is either claiming to be God or committing blasphemy. The teachers recognise this immediately. What they cannot accept is the first option. And so they go straight to the second. Their reaction is not irrational — it is the correct conclusion from a correct premise, but with the wrong assumption about who Jesus is. Their error is not in their theology of forgiveness but in their theology of Jesus.
elements.questionHint: The teachers of the law ask ”who can forgive sins but God alone?” — are they wrong about that? If not, what does their reaction actually reveal about what Jesus is implicitly claiming?

elements.text: When a paralysed man is lowered through a roof by his friends, Jesus doesn’t say what anyone expects. He says, ”Son, your sins are forgiven.” The teachers of the law are immediately on alert. They are right to be: forgiving sins is something only God can do. Jesus is not making a modest claim. Either he is blaspheming, or he is exactly who he is implying he is. He then heals the man to demonstrate that his authority to forgive is real. This is the hinge moment of this section. Jesus doesn’t just have authority over sickness and evil — he has authority over sin itself.

elements.question: What does this reveal about Jesus’ identity? Why is this authority either the most comforting or the most confronting truth in these chapters?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus explicitly claims the title ”Son of Man” in v10 and asserts that the Son of Man has authority to forgive sins on earth. This is a claim to divine authority — the kind of authority that belongs to God alone. The healing of the paralysed man functions as evidence that the claim is real, not empty words. If Jesus can make a paralysed man walk, his claim to forgive sins stands. For someone who knows they are guilty and long to be free, this authority is the most comforting truth imaginable — here is someone who can actually deal with what is wrong at the deepest level. But for someone who has not acknowledged any need for forgiveness, or who feels that Jesus has no right to stand in the place of God, this same authority is deeply confronting: it puts us under his verdict, not the other way around.
elements.questionHint: Jesus uses the healing as evidence for something — look at v10 to see what he says it is meant to prove. Why does the physical miracle demonstrate the spiritual authority?

elements.text: Reflection Questions

elements.question: There is a difference between finding Jesus impressive and being genuinely submitted to him. Which best describes where you are right now?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: Jesus calls people to follow him completely — not admire him from a distance. What areas of your life are you holding back from his authority?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: The paralysed man’s friends went to extraordinary lengths to bring him to Jesus. Is there someone in your life you could be doing more to bring to him?

elements.text: Application Challenge

Identify one concrete area of your life where Jesus’ call to follow has not yet been obeyed. It might be a relationship, a habit, a use of money or time. Bring it to him in prayer, and take one step toward obedience this week.

elements.text:
It is tempting to assume that the people who opposed Jesus were obviously wicked, while those who followed him clearly saw the truth. But Mark’s account is more uncomfortable than that. The opposition to Jesus in these chapters does not come from the obviously irreligious. It comes from the religious — from people who loved God’s law, who attended the synagogue, who cared deeply about right and wrong. And they couldn’t see Jesus for who he was.

This raises an unsettling question: is it possible to be deeply religious and still be blind to what God is actually doing?
Wrong Expectations

elements.passageText: <sup>13</sup>Jesus went out again by the sea. The whole crowd came to him, and he taught them. <sup>14</sup>As he went along, he saw Levi, the son of Alphaeus, sitting at the tax booth. ”Follow me,“ he said to him. And he got up and followed him. <sup>15</sup>As Jesus was having a meal in Levi’s home, many tax collectors and sinners were eating with Jesus and his disciples, for there were many who followed him. <sup>16</sup>When the experts in the law and the Pharisees saw that he was eating with sinners and tax collectors, they said to his disciples, “Why does he eat with tax collectors and sinners?” <sup>17</sup>When Jesus heard this he said to them, “Those who are healthy don’t need a physician, but those who are sick do. I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners.” <sup>18</sup>Now John’s disciples and the Pharisees were fasting. So they came to Jesus and said, “Why do the disciples of John and the disciples of the Pharisees fast, but your disciples don’t fast?” <sup>19</sup>Jesus said to them, “The wedding guests cannot fast while the bridegroom is with them, can they? As long as they have the bridegroom with them they do not fast. <sup>20</sup>But the days are coming when the bridegroom will be taken from them, and at that time they will fast. <sup>21</sup>No one sews a patch of unshrunk cloth on an old garment; otherwise, the patch pulls away from it, the new from the old, and the tear becomes worse. <sup>22</sup>And no one pours new wine into old wineskins; otherwise, the wine will burst the skins, and both the wine and the skins will be destroyed. Instead new wine is poured into new wineskins.”

elements.question: Why do the religious leaders struggle so much with Jesus’ actions?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The Pharisees and experts in the law have a clear framework for what a godly person should look like — and Jesus doesn’t fit it. In v16, they are scandalised that he eats with tax collectors and sinners; table fellowship implied endorsement and acceptance, and no righteous teacher should associate himself with the disreputable. In v18, they are puzzled that his disciples don’t fast — this was an established mark of serious religious devotion. Jesus’s responses (v17, v19–22) show that he is not simply breaking their rules arbitrarily; he is operating from a completely different framework. He has come for the sick, not the well (v17); he is the bridegroom whose presence changes everything (v19); and what he brings cannot be patched onto the old order — it requires entirely new containers (v21–22). Their problem is that they are trying to evaluate a new reality with old categories.
elements.questionHint: Look at Jesus’s own explanation in v17 — what does his image of a doctor and patients reveal about how he sees his mission, and why would that be offensive to people who considered themselves already righteous?

elements.text: Jesus sits down to eat with tax collectors and sinners. This is not a small thing in his culture. To eat with someone was to honour them, to declare fellowship with them. The Pharisees are scandalised. But Jesus is not violating God’s law — he is fulfilling God’s heart. The problem is that they have a clear picture of what a godly man should look like, and Jesus doesn’t match it. Their expectations have become a wall between them and the truth.

elements.question: What does this show about the danger of letting our expectations of God override what God is actually doing?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The Pharisees’ expectations were not trivial or shallow — they were based on genuine theological convictions about purity, holiness, and what it meant to honour God. And yet those very convictions became the thing that prevented them from recognising what God was doing right in front of them. Jesus’s parables of the old and new in v21–22 make the point sharply: you cannot fit the new reality of his coming into your existing religious framework. If you try, something will tear or burst. The danger is that our picture of God — however sincerely held — can become a filter that screens out what he is actually doing when it doesn’t match our expectations. The cure is not to have no convictions, but to hold them with the humility that is willing to be surprised by God.
elements.questionHint: Jesus uses two parables in v21–22 about what happens when you try to combine something new with something old — what goes wrong in each case, and what does that suggest about the religious leaders’ approach to Jesus?

elements.text: Hard Hearts

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Then Jesus entered the synagogue again, and a man was there who had a withered hand. <sup>2</sup>They watched Jesus closely to see if he would heal him on the Sabbath, so that they could accuse him. <sup>3</sup>So he said to the man who had the withered hand, “Stand up among all these people.” <sup>4</sup>Then he said to them, “Is it lawful to do good on the Sabbath, or evil, to save a life or destroy it?” But they were silent. <sup>5</sup>After looking around at them in anger, grieved by the hardness of their hearts, he said to the man, “Stretch out your hand.” He stretched it out, and his hand was restored. <sup>6</sup>So the Pharisees went out immediately and began plotting with the Herodians, as to how they could assassinate him.

elements.question: What is most striking about the Pharisees’ response to Jesus healing in the synagogue?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The most striking thing is that they come not hoping to see a miracle but looking for grounds to accuse Jesus (v2). They are watching closely — but not with open hearts. When Jesus asks them a direct question in v4 — “Is it lawful to do good on the Sabbath, or evil, to save a life or destroy it?” — they are silent. They have no answer that serves their agenda, so they say nothing. And then, immediately after a man is healed and restored, they go out and begin plotting to kill Jesus (v6). Mark records that Jesus is angry and grieved at their ”hardness of heart” (v5). The most extraordinary thing is not their hostility — it is that their hostility is completely unmoved by an act of healing and compassion. Hard hearts can watch grace at work and still respond with murder.
elements.questionHint: Notice what the Pharisees were watching for in v2 compared to what actually happens in v5–6 — what does the gap between those two things reveal about the state of their hearts?

elements.text: A man with a shrivelled hand is there. Jesus heals him. And in response — to an act of compassion and restoration — the Pharisees immediately begin plotting to kill Jesus. Mark records that Jesus looks at them ”with anger and deeply distressed at their stubborn hearts.” This is not a clash of opinion about theology. This is what spiritual blindness looks like at its worst: watching a miracle happen and responding with murderous intent. Hard hearts can watch God at work and see only a threat.

elements.question: How can religion, which is meant to bring us closer to God, actually become a way of resisting him?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The Pharisees in this scene are not godless people — they are in the synagogue on the Sabbath, which is exactly where devout Jews should be. Their concern is with keeping the law, which is not wrong in itself. But their religious observance has become an end in itself rather than a means of knowing God, and it has calcified into a system for judging others. When Jesus challenges their interpretation of the Sabbath with a question about doing good (v4), they can’t engage with it honestly because their goal is not truth but control. Religion can become resistant to God when it turns inward — when keeping the rules replaces loving God, when protecting the institution replaces welcoming the outsider, when theological categories become walls rather than windows. The Pharisees’ very religiosity has made them the people who plot to kill the Son of God.
elements.questionHint: What were the Pharisees trying to protect by watching to accuse Jesus in v2 — and can a concern for protecting something right ever become a reason to oppose what God is doing?

elements.text: Misunderstood by Everyone

elements.passageText: <sup>20</sup>Now Jesus went home, and a crowd gathered so that they were not able to eat. <sup>21</sup>When his family heard this they went out to restrain him, for they said, “He is out of his mind.” <sup>22</sup>The experts in the law who came down from Jerusalem said, “He is possessed by Beelzebul,“ and, “By the ruler of demons he casts out demons.” <sup>23</sup>So he called them and spoke to them in parables: “How can Satan cast out Satan? <sup>24</sup>If a kingdom is divided against itself, that kingdom will not be able to stand. <sup>25</sup>If a house is divided against itself, that house will not be able to stand. <sup>26</sup>And if Satan rises against himself and is divided, he is not able to stand and his end has come. <sup>27</sup>But no one is able to enter a strong man’s house and steal his property unless he first ties up the strong man. Then he can thoroughly plunder his house. <sup>28</sup>I tell you the truth, people will be forgiven for all sins, even all the blasphemies they utter. <sup>29</sup>But whoever blasphemes against the Holy Spirit will never be forgiven, but is guilty of an eternal sin” <sup>30</sup>(because they said, “He has an unclean spirit”). <sup>31</sup>Then Jesus’ mother and his brothers came. Standing outside, they sent word to him, to summon him. <sup>32</sup>A crowd was sitting around him and they said to him, “Look, your mother and your brothers are outside looking for you.” <sup>33</sup>He answered them and said, “Who are my mother and my brothers?” <sup>34</sup>And looking at those who were sitting around him in a circle, he said, “Here are my mother and my brothers! <sup>35</sup>For whoever does the will of God is my brother and sister and mother.”

elements.question: Why do people misinterpret Jesus so badly in Mark 3:20–35?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Two very different groups misinterpret Jesus in this passage, for different reasons. His family think he has ”lost his mind” (v21) — they are looking at his behaviour from the outside, measuring it against normal social expectations, and what they see doesn’t compute. Their assessment is based on observation without understanding. The teachers of the law from Jerusalem come to the opposite but equally wrong conclusion: that his power comes from Beelzebul, the ruler of demons (v22). They see the same evidence — extraordinary power over evil — but rather than acknowledging its divine source, they attribute it to the darkest possible origin. In both cases the misunderstanding stems from a prior framework that cannot accommodate who Jesus actually is. No category available to them — “rabbi,“ “prophet,“ “madman,“ “demoniac” — fits him. They need an entirely new category, and they are not willing to form it.
elements.questionHint: The family and the teachers of the law both misidentify Jesus, but in opposite directions — what does each group’s wrong answer reveal about what they are using as their measuring stick?

elements.text: By this point, even Jesus’ family thinks he has lost his mind. The teachers of the law from Jerusalem conclude he is empowered by the devil. Not strangers — his own family and the nation’s religious scholars. It would be easy to dismiss these people as foolish or malicious. But Mark seems to want us to feel the weight of it: if even those closest to him misunderstood him this badly, how confident should we be that we are seeing him clearly?

elements.question: What does this pattern of misunderstanding reveal about human nature? What is the cure?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The pattern of misunderstanding across this passage reveals that proximity to Jesus is not enough to produce understanding, and that intelligence or religious learning is no guarantee either. Family closeness and theological expertise both fail. What is needed is something different: a willingness to do the will of God (v35). Jesus redefines his true family not as those who are physically near or religiously educated, but as those who do God’s will. The cure for misunderstanding Jesus is not more information — it is a posture of submission and trust. Those who are genuinely seeking to do what God asks are in a position to recognise who Jesus is; those who are primarily defending their own position or applying their own framework will keep getting him wrong.
elements.questionHint: How does Jesus redefine ”family” in v34–35 — and what does his definition suggest about what it actually takes to understand and belong to him?

elements.text: Reflection Questions

elements.question: The religious leaders were sincere people who thought they were serving God — and yet they missed him completely. Does this concern you? Why or why not?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: Are there ways you have constructed a picture of Jesus that is more comfortable than the real thing?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: ”True belonging,“ Jesus says, means doing the will of God (3:35). How does that challenge the way you think about your relationship with him?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: Where might you be resisting Jesus not because of wickedness, but simply because he is challenging something you hold dear?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.text: Application Challenge
Take a piece of paper and write down the question: “Where am I resisting Jesus — and why?” Sit with it honestly. Don’t rush to a comfortable answer. Then bring what you have written to him in prayer.

elements.question: It’s worth reading the whole passage before you begin...

elements.text:
Have you ever read a passage of the Bible many times and suddenly, one day, it means something entirely different to you — not because the words changed, but because you did? That experience points to something Jesus teaches directly in this chapter. The problem with understanding God’s word is not usually a problem with the word. It is a problem with the listener.

Jesus teaches large crowds in parables — vivid, earthy stories drawn from everyday life. And he does this, he says, because ”seeing, they may not see; and hearing, they may not understand.” This is challenging. Jesus is not saying his teaching is unclear. He is saying that the condition of the heart determines whether the message gets through.
The Parable of the Sower

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Again he began to teach by the lake. Such a large crowd gathered around him that he got into a boat on the lake and sat there while the whole crowd was on the shore by the lake. <sup>2</sup>He taught them many things in parables, and in his teaching said to them: <sup>3”Listen! A sower went out to sow. <sup>4</sup>And as he sowed, some seed fell along the path, and the birds came and devoured it. <sup>5</sup>Other seed fell on rocky ground where it did not have much soil. It sprang up at once because the soil was not deep. <sup>6</sup>When the sun came up it was scorched, and because it did not have sufficient root, it withered. <sup>7</sup>Other seed fell among the thorns, and they grew up and choked it, and it did not produce grain. <sup>8</sup>But other seed fell on good soil and produced grain, sprouting and growing; some yielded thirty times as much, some sixty, and some a hundred times.” <sup>9</sup>And he said, “Whoever has ears to hear had better listen!” <sup>10</sup>When he was alone, those around him with the twelve asked him about the parables. <sup>11</sup>He said to them, “The secret of the kingdom of God has been given to you. But to those outside, everything is in parables, <sup>12</sup>so that although they look they may look but not see, and although they hear they may hear but not understand, so they may not repent and be forgiven.” <sup>13</sup>He said to them, “Don’t you understand this parable? Then how will you understand any parable? <sup>14</sup>The sower sows the word. <sup>15</sup>These are the ones on the path where the word is sown: Whenever they hear, immediately Satan comes and snatches the word that was sown in them. <sup>16</sup>These are the ones sown on rocky ground: As soon as they hear the word, they receive it with joy. <sup>17</sup>But they have no root in themselves and do not endure. Then, when trouble or persecution comes because of the word, immediately they fall away. <sup>18</sup>Others are the ones sown among thorns: They are those who hear the word, <sup>19</sup>but worldly cares, the seductiveness of wealth, and the desire for other things come in and choke the word, and it produces nothing. <sup>20</sup>But these are the ones sown on good soil: They hear the word and receive it and bear fruit, one thirty times as much, one sixty, and one a hundred times.

elements.question: Which of the four soils do you think best describes your life right now?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: This question is deliberately personal, but the passage gives us the categories to work with. The path (v15) describes a heart where the word doesn’t even begin to take hold — Satan snatches it away immediately. The rocky ground (v16–17) describes someone who responds with initial enthusiasm but has no real depth; the first sign of cost or difficulty causes them to fall away. The thorny ground (v18–19) describes someone where the word begins to grow but is crowded out by ”worldly cares, the seductiveness of wealth, and the desire for other things” — not dramatic rejection, just slow suffocation by competing priorities. The good soil (v20) is the person who hears the word, receives it, and bears fruit — in varying amounts, but genuinely. Honest self-examination against these categories is the point.
elements.questionHint: Look carefully at what Jesus says stops growth in each of the three unsuccessful soils — are they all the same kind of obstacle, or do they represent different kinds of problems?

elements.text: A farmer scatters seed on four kinds of ground. The seed is the same in every case — the same word, the same message. What differs is the soil. Some seed is snatched away before it takes root. Some springs up quickly but has no depth and withers. Some grows but is choked out by competing concerns. And some falls on good soil and produces an extraordinary harvest. Jesus is not describing four different kinds of people out there. He is holding up a mirror.

elements.question: What stops growth in each case? Which ”threat to growth” do you find most recognisable in your own life?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: In the path soil (v15), Satan acts immediately to snatch the word away — the threat is external and aggressive, a direct removal of what was received. In the rocky soil (v16–17), growth begins but there is no root; when trouble or persecution comes because of the word, the person falls away — the threat is external pressure and the cost of commitment. In the thorny soil (v18–19), the threat is more insidious: “worldly cares, the seductiveness of wealth, and the desire for other things” crowd in and gradually choke growth without any dramatic moment of rejection. Of these three, the thorny soil threat is probably the most recognisable in comfortable or busy lives — not outright opposition but the slow suffocation of the word by the competing attractions of an ordinary life.
elements.questionHint: Jesus is specific about what causes the thorny soil to fail — list the three things he names in v19. Are any of them obviously bad things, or is that part of what makes them so dangerous?

elements.text: Why Parables?

elements.passageText: <sup>10</sup>When he was alone, those around him with the twelve asked him about the parables. <sup>11</sup>He said to them, “The secret of the kingdom of God has been given to you. But to those outside, everything is in parables, <sup>12</sup>so that although they look they may look but not see, and although they hear they may hear but not understand, so they may not repent and be forgiven.”

elements.question: Why doesn’t everyone understand the parables in the same way?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus gives a striking answer in v11–12: the secret of the kingdom has been ”given” to those around him, while for those outside everything remains in parables — and the result is that they look without seeing and hear without understanding. This is deliberately confronting. The parables are not simply a teaching method to help everyone understand more easily — they function differently for different kinds of hearers. Those who are genuinely seeking, who press in and ask (as the disciples do in v10), receive the explanation. Those who are merely curious, or resistant, or satisfied with their own understanding, do not press in — and the story stays at the surface level. The parables reveal the condition of the listener as much as they reveal the truth about the kingdom.
elements.questionHint: Notice who asks Jesus about the parables in v10 — what did they have to do to get an explanation, and what does that action suggest about the kind of person who understands?

elements.text: When the disciples ask Jesus privately about the parables, he gives a striking answer. The parables are not simply illustrations to make things clearer — they are a kind of test. Those who are genuinely open and seeking come closer; those who are closed find the story lands lightly and passes on. This is not cruelty on Jesus’ part. It is a description of how spiritual perception works. A person who is genuinely hungry for truth will press in. A person who is satisfied with their current understanding will not.

elements.question: What does this teach us about the posture we need to bring to God’s word?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The disciples’ behaviour in v10 is the model: when alone with Jesus, they ask him about the parables. They don’t assume they already understand. They don’t walk away satisfied with the surface story. They press in and ask. Jesus responds by giving them the secret of the kingdom. The posture this implies is one of genuine hunger and humility — the recognition that you haven’t yet understood, combined with the willingness to ask. It also implies a willingness to be changed by what you hear, since understanding the parable leads toward repentance and forgiveness (v12). Bringing this posture to God’s word means coming not to confirm what you already believe but to be genuinely confronted and reshaped.
elements.questionHint: Compare the behaviour of those who are ”outside” in v11 with those who ask Jesus in v10 — what is the practical difference between those two groups, and which one finds the parables making sense?

elements.text: The Growing Kingdom

elements.passageText: <sup>26</sup>He also said, “The kingdom of God is like someone who spreads seed on the ground. <sup>27</sup>He goes to sleep and gets up, night and day, and the seed sprouts and grows, though he does not know how. <sup>28</sup>By itself the soil produces a crop, first the stalk, then the head, then the full grain in the head. <sup>29</sup>And when the grain is ripe, he sends in the sickle because the harvest has come.” <sup>30</sup>He also asked, “To what can we compare the kingdom of God, or what parable can we use to present it? <sup>31</sup>It is like a mustard seed that when sown in the ground, even though it is the smallest of all the seeds in the ground — <sup>32</sup>when it is sown, it grows up, becomes the greatest of all garden plants, and grows large branches so that the wild birds can nest in its shade.” <sup>33</sup>So with many parables like these, he spoke the word to them, as they were able to hear. <sup>34</sup>He did not speak to them without a parable. But privately he explained everything to his own disciples.

elements.question: What encourages you about how Jesus describes the growth of God’s kingdom?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Two things stand out as encouragements. First, the growth of the kingdom is not dependent on human understanding or effort. The farmer in v27–28 goes to sleep, gets up, and watches the seed grow — “though he does not know how.” The soil produces the crop by itself. This is deeply reassuring: the kingdom grows by God’s own power, not by our programmes or strategies. Second, the mustard seed parable (v31–32) emphasises the extraordinary disproportionality between beginning and end. What starts as the smallest of seeds becomes a great plant where birds nest. The apparently insignificant beginnings of the kingdom — a wandering teacher in Galilee — are no indication of its ultimate scope and reach. Both parables encourage patience and trust in a growth process that is larger than we can see or control.
elements.questionHint: In the parable of the growing seed, what is the farmer actually doing while the crop grows — and what does that suggest about the source of the kingdom’s growth?

elements.text: The farmer scatters seed — and then goes to sleep. He doesn’t understand how growth happens. He simply trusts that it will. The seed does its work in the dark. And the result — a harvest from a tiny beginning — is out of all proportion to what seemed possible at the start. This is how God’s kingdom works. It is not always visible. It does not always feel dramatic. But it is growing.

elements.question: Why might trusting this pattern of growth be difficult? What would it look like to trust it more fully?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Trusting this pattern of growth is difficult for several reasons. The mustard seed parable begins with smallness and hiddenness — the kingdom does not look impressive at the start, or even for a long time. In a culture that measures success by visible results and immediate impact, a slow and unspectacular growth process can feel like failure. The farmer in v27 ”does not know how” the seed grows — he has to live with mystery and a lack of control, which is uncomfortable. Trusting it more fully might mean: faithfully sowing the word without demanding to see immediate results; investing in things that seem small (a conversation, a prayer, a child’s faith) without discounting them; and resisting the temptation to abandon slow work for something that feels more dramatic. The harvest is certain — the timing and the mechanisms are in God’s hands.
elements.questionHint: The farmer in v27 is described as not knowing how growth happens — does that stop him from sowing? What does his behaviour model about living with the mystery of God’s growth?

elements.text: Reflection Questions

elements.question: When you read the Bible or hear God’s word, what kind of ”soil” do you tend to be? What distractions or pressures do you think most often choke growth in your life?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: Are you genuinely open to being challenged and changed by what God’s word says — or do you tend to listen for confirmation of what you already think?

elements.question: The growing kingdom is largely hidden and slow. Does that comfort you or frustrate you? What does your answer reveal?

elements.question: What practices help you to hear God’s word well — and which habits make it harder?
