elements.text: Application Challenge

Set aside one extended, distraction-free period this week — phone away, no background noise — to read a passage of Scripture slowly and prayerfully. Ask God before you start: “What do you want me to see?” Notice what comes up.

elements.question: It’s worth reading the whole passage before you begin...

elements.text:
Suppose someone you trusted promised to take care of you — and then, the very next day, something frightening happened. Would you trust the promise, or would the fear take over? Most of us know the answer from experience. When fear is immediate and the promise feels distant, fear usually wins. That is exactly what happens to the disciples in this section of Mark. They have seen Jesus at work. They have heard him teach. And when a storm hits the boat, they panic.

Jesus’ response is not primarily a lesson about weather. It is a question aimed straight at the heart: ”Do you still have no faith?”
Fear vs Faith

elements.passageText: <sup>35</sup>On that day, when evening came, Jesus said to his disciples, “Let’s go across to the other side of the lake.” <sup>36</sup>So after leaving the crowd, they took him along, just as he was, in the boat, and other boats were with him. <sup>37</sup>Now a great windstorm developed and the waves were breaking into the boat, so that the boat was nearly swamped. <sup>38</sup>But he was in the stern, sleeping on a cushion. They woke him up and said to him, “Teacher, don’t you care that we are about to die?” <sup>39</sup>So he got up and rebuked the wind, and said to the sea, “Be quiet! Calm down!” Then the wind stopped, and it was dead calm. <sup>40</sup>And he said to them, “Why are you cowardly? Do you still not have faith?” <sup>41</sup>They were overwhelmed by fear and said to one another, “Who then is this? Even the wind and sea obey him!”

elements.question: What does Jesus’ response to the storm reveal about what he expects from his disciples?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus’ two questions — “Why are you cowardly?” and ”Do you still not have faith?” — reveal that he expects his disciples to trust him even in the middle of a crisis. The word ”still” is significant: it implies that by this point they should already have developed faith, based on what they had seen and heard. He is not surprised that the storm is frightening; he is surprised that fear displaced trust entirely. His expectation is not that they feel no fear, but that their confidence in him would hold.
elements.questionHint: Notice the two questions Jesus asks after he calms the storm — what do they suggest he thought the disciples should have already known?

elements.text: The storm is real. The danger is real. The disciples’ fear is entirely understandable. What is striking is that Jesus is asleep in the stern — not oblivious, but at rest. When they wake him in a panic, he calms the storm with a word and then turns to them with a question. Not ”Are you alright?” but ”Why are you so afraid? Do you still have no faith?” The implication is that they had everything they needed to trust him — they just didn’t.

elements.question: What had the disciples already seen and heard that should have given them grounds for trust? What gets in the way of faith even when we have good reasons for it?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: By this point, the disciples had witnessed Jesus heal the sick, cast out demons, and teach with extraordinary authority. They had heard his parables about the kingdom of God and seen crowds amazed at his words. They had every reason to believe he was no ordinary man. Yet when the storm hit, none of that seemed to matter — the immediate danger overwhelmed their memory of what they had already seen. This is a pattern most of us recognise: fear is visceral and present, while past evidence of God’s faithfulness can feel distant and abstract in a crisis. Pride, self-reliance, and the sheer intensity of circumstances all conspire against faith even when we have solid grounds for it.
elements.questionHint: Think back through the chapters of Mark leading up to this moment — what had Jesus already done that the disciples were present for?

elements.text: Power Over Evil and Death

elements.passageText:
1So they came to the other side of the lake, to the region of the Gerasenes. 2Just as Jesus was getting out of the boat, a man with an unclean spirit came from the tombs and met him. 3He lived among the tombs, and no one could bind him anymore, not even with a chain. 4For his hands and feet had often been bound with chains and shackles, but he had torn the chains apart and broken the shackles in pieces. No one was strong enough to subdue him. 5Each night and every day among the tombs and in the mountains, he would cry out and cut himself with stones. 6When he saw Jesus from a distance, he ran and bowed down before him. 7Then he cried out with a loud voice, “Leave me alone, Jesus, Son of the Most High God! I implore you by God—do not torment me!” 8(For Jesus had said to him, “Come out of that man, you unclean spirit!”) 9Jesus asked him, “What is your name?” And he said, “My name is Legion, for we are many.” 10He begged Jesus repeatedly not to send them out of the region. 11There on the hillside, a great herd of pigs was feeding. 12And the demonic spirits begged him, “Send us into the pigs. Let us enter them.” 13Jesus gave them permission. So the unclean spirits came out and went into the pigs. Then the herd rushed down the steep slope into the lake, and about 2,000 were drowned in the lake.

25Now a woman was there who had been suffering from a hemorrhage for 12 years. 26She had endured a great deal under the care of many doctors and had spent all that she had. Yet instead of getting better, she grew worse. 27When she heard about Jesus, she came up behind him in the crowd and touched his cloak, 28for she kept saying, “If only I touch his clothes, I will be healed.” 29At once the bleeding stopped, and she felt in her body that she was healed of her disease. 30Jesus knew at once that power had gone out from him. He turned around in the crowd and said, “Who touched my clothes?” 31His disciples said to him, “You see the crowd pressing against you and you say, ‘Who touched me?’” 32But he looked around to see who had done it. 33Then the woman, with fear and trembling, knowing what had happened to her, came and fell down before him and told him the whole truth. 34He said to her, “Daughter, your faith has made you well. Go in peace, and be healed of your disease.”

35While he was still speaking, people came from the synagogue leader’s house saying, “Your daughter has died. Why trouble the teacher any longer?” 36But Jesus, paying no attention to what was said, told the synagogue leader, “Do not be afraid; just believe.” 37He did not let anyone follow him except Peter, James, and John, the brother of James. 38They came to the house of the synagogue leader where he saw noisy confusion and people weeping and wailing loudly. 39When he entered he said to them, “Why are you distressed and weeping? The child is not dead but asleep!” 40And they began making fun of him. But he forced them all outside, and he took the child’s father and mother and his own companions and went into the room where the child was. 41Then, gently taking the child by the hand, he said to her, “Talitha koum,“ which means, “Little girl, I say to you, get up.” 42The girl got up at once and began to walk around (she was twelve years old). They were completely astonished at this.

elements.question: What do the miracles in Mark 5 reveal about who Jesus is?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The three miracles together paint a portrait of Jesus as someone with authority over every domain of human suffering and lostness. The exorcism of Legion shows he has power over evil — even a force so overwhelming that no human restraint could contain it bows completely before him. The healing of the bleeding woman shows he has power over long-term physical suffering that has defeated every human remedy. The raising of Jairus’s daughter shows he has power over death itself. What is also striking is that the demonic spirits recognise him immediately as ”Son of the Most High God” — a title that reveals his divine identity even before the disciples fully grasp it.
elements.questionHint: Think about what each of the three miracles has in common — what kinds of power or forces does Jesus demonstrate authority over?

elements.text: In a single chapter, Jesus confronts a man so tormented by evil that no chains could hold him — and delivers him completely. He heals a woman who had been suffering for twelve years and had exhausted every human remedy. And he raises a twelve-year-old girl from death. These are not three random miracles. They are a comprehensive demonstration. There is no darkness Jesus cannot enter. There is no suffering beyond his reach. There is no door, including death’s, that he cannot open.

elements.question: Why do people still struggle to trust him, even after seeing this?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The passage itself offers clues. The crowd in the region of the Gerasenes, having witnessed the exorcism of Legion, respond not with faith but with fear — and they ask Jesus to leave. Even Jairus, who comes to Jesus in desperate faith, faces the moment when messengers arrive saying his daughter has died and there is no point troubling Jesus further. The pull toward despair, the sense that the situation has passed the point where even Jesus can help, is powerful. The passage suggests that familiarity with Jesus’ power does not automatically produce trust — something deeper is required, something Jesus himself calls faith. Our tendency to trust what we can see and control, rather than what we know of Jesus, keeps reasserting itself.
elements.questionHint: Look at how the people around Jesus respond after each miracle — do they all respond with faith, and what does that tell you?

elements.text: Familiarity Breeds Unbelief

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Now Jesus left that place and came to his hometown, and his disciples followed him. <sup>2</sup>When the Sabbath came, he began to teach in the synagogue. Many who heard him were astonished, saying, “Where did he get these ideas? And what is this wisdom that has been given to him? What are these miracles that are done through his hands? <sup>3</sup>Isn’t this the carpenter, the son of Mary and brother of James, Joses, Judas, and Simon? And aren’t his sisters here with us?” And so they took offense at him. <sup>4</sup>Then Jesus said to them, “A prophet is not without honor except in his hometown, and among his relatives, and in his own house.” <sup>5</sup>He was not able to do a miracle there, except to lay his hands on a few sick people and heal them. <sup>6</sup>And he was amazed because of their unbelief. Then he went around among the villages and taught.

elements.question: How does the response of Jesus’ hometown illustrate a particular kind of spiritual danger?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The people of Nazareth are not hostile in the way the religious leaders are — they are impressed, even astonished, by Jesus’ wisdom and miracles. But their familiarity with him as a local tradesman becomes a barrier. Instead of letting what they hear and see lead them to reconsider who he is, they allow their existing category for him — “the carpenter, Mary’s son” — to override the evidence. They take offence rather than take faith. The spiritual danger illustrated here is the assumption that we already know enough. Familiarity creates a sense of certainty that can close a person off to deeper truth. It is possible to be close to Jesus in outward terms — knowing the stories, the language, the community — and still miss him entirely.
elements.questionHint: What is it that the people of Nazareth use to dismiss Jesus, and why is that particular thing so dangerous for someone who has grown up around faith?

elements.text: Jesus returns to Nazareth. These are people who have known him since childhood — watched him grow up, worked alongside his family. And precisely because they think they know him, they cannot receive him. ”Isn’t this the carpenter? Isn’t this Mary’s son?” They are so sure they have him figured out that they cannot see who he really is. Mark notes something remarkable: Jesus ”could not do many miracles there” — not because his power was limited, but because their unbelief closed the door.

elements.question: How might familiarity with Jesus — or with church, or with Christian language — lead to a kind of comfortable blindness today?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: In Nazareth, it was physical proximity and shared history that dulled perception. The equivalent today might be growing up in a Christian home, attending church for years, or being fluent in theological language — all without ever being genuinely confronted by Jesus as he actually is. Someone in that position may hear the same words week after week and filter them through the assumption that they already understand. The language becomes familiar; the categories feel settled. But real encounter with Jesus tends to be unsettling and demanding. Comfortable familiarity — with the music, the community, the vocabulary — can substitute for actual faith, in the same way that knowing someone’s family background is not the same as knowing them.
elements.questionHint: What was it that made the people of Nazareth so certain they understood Jesus — and what modern equivalent might produce the same closed-off response?

elements.text: Reflection Questions

elements.question: What fears, right now, are competing with your faith? Have you brought them honestly to Jesus, or have you been trying to manage them yourself?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: The disciples had seen remarkable things and still panicked in the storm. Does that encourage you or concern you? Why?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: Jesus’ hometown knew him — and missed him. Is there a danger that you have become so used to Jesus that you have stopped really seeing him?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: If Jesus has authority over evil, suffering, and death, what does that mean for the specific situation you are facing right now?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.text: Application Challenge

Identify one specific fear that is weighing on you this week. Each day, bring it to Jesus in prayer — not just mentioning it, but deliberately placing it in his hands. Notice whether anything changes, not in the circumstances, but in you.

elements.question: It’s worth reading the whole passage before you begin...

elements.text:
Every story has a hinge — a moment when everything turns. In Mark’s Gospel, that moment is here. Jesus and his disciples are walking through the villages near Caesarea Philippi, and Jesus asks them two questions. The first is warm-up: “Who do people say I am?” The second is the one that counts: “But what about you — who do you say I am?”

That second question does not stay safely in the first century. It comes forward through the pages of the Gospel and lands directly in front of every reader. You cannot stay neutral on it. The question demands an answer.
Who Do You Say I Am?

elements.question: Why is this question the central question of Mark’s Gospel — and of the Christian life?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Mark’s entire Gospel has been building toward this moment. From chapter one, where Jesus is announced as the Son of God and the Spirit descends on him, to the succession of miracles and controversies, the implicit question running through every scene is: who is this man? Now Jesus asks it directly — not about what crowds think, but about what his closest followers personally believe. The Christian life is not primarily about following a moral code or joining a community; it is grounded in a particular answer to this question. Everything else — how you live, what you hope for, how you face death — flows from who you say Jesus is. A vague or borrowed answer is not enough; the question is addressed to each person individually.
elements.questionHint: Notice that Jesus asks two versions of the question — why do you think he distinguishes between what others say and what the disciples themselves say?

elements.passageText: <sup>27</sup>Then Jesus and his disciples went out to the villages of Caesarea Philippi. On the way he asked his disciples, “Who do people say that I am?” <sup>28</sup>They said, “John the Baptist, others say Elijah, and still others, one of the prophets.” <sup>29</sup>He asked them, “But who do you say that I am?” Peter answered him, “You are the Christ.” <sup>30</sup>Then he warned them not to tell anyone about him.

elements.text: Peter answers immediately and correctly: “You are the Messiah.” It is the right answer. It is the answer the whole Gospel has been building toward. But as the very next section shows, getting the title right is not the same as understanding what it means. Peter knows who Jesus is — but he is about to show that he has badly misunderstood why Jesus has come.

elements.question: How would you answer Jesus’ question today, in your own words?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus is the Christ — the promised Messiah, God’s appointed King who came not to conquer by force but to rescue his people through his death and resurrection. He is fully human, born into history, and yet the one in whom God’s own authority and identity are present. He is the one who has the right to call me to follow him, and the one in whom my only real hope rests. He is not simply a teacher or a moral example; he is the living Lord to whom I owe my whole life.
elements.questionHint: Peter gave a title — can you put into your own words what you personally believe about Jesus, beyond just repeating a name or label?

elements.text: A Shocking Mission

elements.question: Why does Peter react so strongly to Jesus’ prediction of his suffering?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Peter has just identified Jesus as the Messiah — a title that in Jewish expectation carried connotations of power, victory, and restoration. The Messiah was supposed to triumph over Israel’s enemies, not be handed over to them. When Jesus immediately begins describing rejection, suffering, and death, it directly contradicts Peter’s understanding of what messiahship means. Peter’s reaction is not simply emotional distress; it reflects a deeply held theological assumption about how God’s chosen king should act. To rebuke Jesus is to insist that this cannot be the right path — that there must be a better way, one that looks more like winning.
elements.questionHint: What would a first-century Jew have expected the Messiah to do — and how does Jesus’ description of his mission contradict that expectation?

elements.passageText: <sup>31</sup>Then Jesus began to teach them that the Son of Man must suffer many things and be rejected by the elders, chief priests, and experts in the law, and be killed, and after three days rise again. <sup>32</sup>He spoke openly about this. So Peter took him aside and began to rebuke him. <sup>33</sup>But after turning and looking at his disciples, he rebuked Peter and said, “Get behind me, Satan. You are not setting your mind on God’s interests, but on man’s.”

elements.text: Jesus begins to explain what being the Messiah actually involves: rejection, suffering, death, and then resurrection. Peter cannot accept it. He takes Jesus aside and rebukes him. This is not a small moment of doubt — it is a direct challenge to Jesus’ understanding of his own mission. And Jesus’ response is fierce: ”Get behind me, Satan!” Peter has got the identity right but the mission completely wrong. He is imagining a Messiah shaped by human hopes — a conqueror, not a servant; a throne, not a cross.

elements.question: What does Peter’s misunderstanding reveal about the way we tend to imagine God working? Where do you see similar thinking in yourself?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Peter’s error is essentially that he is ”setting his mind on the things of man” rather than ”the things of God.” He assumes God works through strength, success, and the avoidance of suffering — that a true king does not suffer defeat. This is a deeply human instinct. We tend to look for God’s blessing in the form of health, prosperity, and outcomes that look like winning. When suffering or failure comes, we can easily interpret it as evidence that something has gone wrong, that God is absent, or that we have made a mistake. The cross inverts this entirely: it shows that God’s greatest work was accomplished through what looked like total defeat. Most of us, honestly, find this pattern as difficult to accept as Peter did.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says Peter is thinking about ”the things of man” rather than ”the things of God” — what does that contrast suggest about our default assumptions regarding how God should act?

elements.text: The Call to Discipleship

elements.question: What does Jesus mean when he calls his followers to ”take up their cross”?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: For Jesus’ first hearers, a cross was not a symbol of spirituality — it was an instrument of execution. A person carrying their own cross was someone walking to their death, publicly surrendering any claim to their own life. Jesus uses this image deliberately: to follow him means ceasing to live as the sovereign director of your own life. It means accepting that your agenda, your comfort, your reputation, and even your safety are no longer the supreme concern. In context, Jesus has just described his own path of suffering and rejection; the call to take up a cross is the call to follow him on that same kind of path, whatever the specific cost turns out to be for each person.
elements.questionHint: What did it mean in the Roman world to literally carry a cross — and what does that original meaning add to Jesus’ call in verse 34?

elements.passageText: <sup>34</sup>Then Jesus called the crowd, along with his disciples, and said to them, “If anyone wants to become my follower, he must deny himself, take up his cross, and follow me. <sup>35</sup>For whoever wants to save his life will lose it, but whoever loses his life for my sake and for the gospel will save it. <sup>36</sup>For what does it benefit a person to gain the whole world, yet forfeit his life? <sup>37</sup>What can a person give in exchange for his life? <sup>38</sup>For if anyone is ashamed of me and my words in this adulterous and sinful generation, the Son of Man will also be ashamed of him when he comes in the glory of his Father with the holy angels.”

elements.text: Having spoken about his own path of suffering, Jesus turns to the crowd and explains what following him involves. ”Whoever wants to be my disciple must deny themselves and take up their cross and follow me.” This is not an invitation to a comfortable life with Jesus as a helpful addition. It is a call to reorient your entire existence around him — to stop living for yourself, and to start living for him, whatever the cost.

elements.question: Why is this call so difficult? What would it mean to take it seriously?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The call is difficult because it runs directly against the grain of self-preservation, which is one of the strongest human instincts. Jesus does not minimise this tension — he names it explicitly: “whoever wants to save his life will lose it.” We naturally want to protect our security, social standing, comfort, and future. The call to deny yourself and follow Jesus asks you to subordinate all of those things to him. To take it seriously would mean examining honestly where self-protection or self-advancement is actually driving your decisions, and being willing to let those concerns be overridden by what it means to follow Jesus — including in areas like relationships, money, reputation, and time.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says that trying to save your life leads to losing it, but losing your life for him leads to saving it — what does that paradox tell you about why the call is so costly?

elements.text: Reflection Questions

elements.question: Peter got the right answer about Jesus’ identity but completely misunderstood his mission. Is it possible to have correct beliefs about Jesus while still fundamentally misunderstanding what following him involves?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: Do you find it easy or difficult to accept a Messiah who suffers? What does your answer reveal about your expectations of God?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: Jesus says that whoever tries to save their life will lose it, but whoever loses their life for him will save it. What does ”losing your life” for Jesus look like in your specific situation?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: Where in your life are you avoiding the cost of following Jesus? What would it look like to stop avoiding it?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.text: Application Challenge

Identify one specific ”cost” of following Jesus that you have been reluctant to embrace — a sacrifice, a change in behaviour, a conversation you have been avoiding. This week, take one concrete step toward embracing it. Bring it to God in prayer first.

chapters.chapterTitle: The Cross and the Empty Tomb

elements.question: It’s worth reading the whole passage before you begin...
elements.answer:
15:1Early in the morning, after forming a plan, the chief priests with the elders and the experts in the law and the whole Sanhedrin tied Jesus up, led him away, and handed him over to Pilate. 2So Pilate asked him, “Are you the king of the Jews?” He replied, “You say so.” 3Then the chief priests began to accuse him repeatedly. 4So Pilate asked him again, “Have you nothing to say? See how many charges they are bringing against you!” 5But Jesus made no further reply, so that Pilate was amazed.

6During the feast it was customary to release one prisoner to the people, whomever they requested. 7A man named Barabbas was imprisoned with rebels who had committed murder during an insurrection. 8Then the crowd came up and began to ask Pilate to release a prisoner for them, as was his custom. 9So Pilate asked them, “Do you want me to release the king of the Jews for you?” 10(For he knew that the chief priests had handed him over because of envy.) 11But the chief priests stirred up the crowd to have him release Barabbas instead. 12So Pilate spoke to them again, “Then what do you want me to do with the one you call king of the Jews?” 13They shouted back, “Crucify him!” 14Pilate asked them, “Why? What has he done wrong?” But they shouted more insistently, “Crucify him!” 15Because he wanted to satisfy the crowd, Pilate released Barabbas for them. Then, after he had Jesus flogged, he handed him over to be crucified.

16So the soldiers led him into the palace (that is, the governor’s residence) and called together the whole cohort. 17They put a purple cloak on him and after braiding a crown of thorns, they put it on him. 18They began to salute him: “Hail, king of the Jews!” 19Again and again they struck him on the head with a staff and spit on him. Then they knelt down and paid homage to him. 20When they had finished mocking him, they stripped him of the purple cloak and put his own clothes back on him. Then they led him away to crucify him.

21The soldiers forced a passerby to carry his cross, Simon of Cyrene, who was coming in from the country (he was the father of Alexander and Rufus). 22They brought Jesus to a place called Golgotha (which is translated, “Place of the Skull”). 23They offered him wine mixed with myrrh, but he did not take it. 24Then they crucified him and divided his clothes, throwing dice for them, to decide what each would take. 25It was nine o’clock in the morning when they crucified him. 26The inscription of the charge against him read, “The king of the Jews.” 27And they crucified two outlaws with him, one on his right and one on his left. 29Those who passed by defamed him, shaking their heads and saying, “Aha! You who can destroy the temple and rebuild it in three days, 30save yourself and come down from the cross!” 31In the same way even the chief priests—together with the experts in the law—were mocking him among themselves: “He saved others, but he cannot save himself! 32Let the Christ, the king of Israel, come down from the cross now, that we may see and believe!” Those who were crucified with him also spoke abusively to him.

33Now when it was noon, darkness came over the whole land until three in the afternoon. 34Around three o’clock Jesus cried out with a loud voice, “Eloi, Eloi, lema sabachthani?” which means, “My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?” 35When some of the bystanders heard it they said, “Listen, he is calling for Elijah!” 36Then someone ran, filled a sponge with sour wine, put it on a stick, and gave it to him to drink, saying, “Leave him alone! Let’s see if Elijah will come to take him down!” 37But Jesus cried out with a loud voice and breathed his last. 38And the temple curtain was torn in two, from top to bottom. 39Now when the centurion, who stood in front of him, saw how he died, he said, “Truly this man was God’s Son!” 40There were also women, watching from a distance. Among them were Mary Magdalene, and Mary the mother of James the younger and of Joses, and Salome. 41When he was in Galilee, they had followed him and given him support. Many other women who had come up with him to Jerusalem were there too.

42Now when evening had already come, since it was the day of preparation (that is, the day before the Sabbath), 43Joseph of Arimathea, a highly regarded member of the council, who was himself looking forward to the kingdom of God, went boldly to Pilate and asked for the body of Jesus. 44Pilate was surprised that he was already dead. He called the centurion and asked him if he had been dead for some time. 45When Pilate was informed by the centurion, he gave the body to Joseph. 46After Joseph bought a linen cloth and took down the body, he wrapped it in the linen and placed it in a tomb cut out of the rock. Then he rolled a stone across the entrance of the tomb. 47Mary Magdalene and Mary the mother of Joses saw where the body was placed.16:1When the Sabbath was over, Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James, and Salome bought aromatic spices so that they might go and anoint him. 2And very early on the first day of the week, at sunrise, they went to the tomb. 3They had been asking each other, “Who will roll away the stone for us from the entrance to the tomb?” 4But when they looked up, they saw that the stone, which was very large, had been rolled back. 5Then as they went into the tomb, they saw a young man dressed in a white robe sitting on the right side; and they were alarmed. 6But he said to them, “Do not be alarmed. You are looking for Jesus the Nazarene, who was crucified. He has been raised! He is not here. Look, there is the place where they laid him. 7But go, tell his disciples, even Peter, that he is going ahead of you into Galilee. 8You will see him there, just as he told you.” Then they went out and ran from the tomb, for terror and bewilderment had seized them. And they said nothing to anyone, because they were afraid.

elements.text:
When Roman soldiers crucified Jesus, everyone — friend and enemy alike — would have seen the same thing: a man who had claimed great things, dying in humiliation. His enemies would have seen it as confirmation that they were right to oppose him. His followers would have felt their hopes collapse. The cross looked like the end of the story.

But Mark has been preparing us to read it differently. From the very first verse, he has been telling us who Jesus is. And now, as the story reaches its apparent low point, the truth breaks through in the most unexpected place — on the lips of a Roman soldier.
The Crucifixion

elements.passageText:
16So the soldiers led him into the palace (that is, the governor’s residence) and called together the whole cohort. 17They put a purple cloak on him and after braiding a crown of thorns, they put it on him. 18They began to salute him: “Hail, king of the Jews!” 19Again and again they struck him on the head with a staff and spit on him. Then they knelt down and paid homage to him. 20When they had finished mocking him, they stripped him of the purple cloak and put his own clothes back on him. Then they led him away to crucify him.

21The soldiers forced a passerby to carry his cross, Simon of Cyrene, who was coming in from the country (he was the father of Alexander and Rufus). 22They brought Jesus to a place called Golgotha (which is translated, “Place of the Skull”). 23They offered him wine mixed with myrrh, but he did not take it. 24Then they crucified him and divided his clothes, throwing dice for them, to decide what each would take. 25It was nine o’clock in the morning when they crucified him. 26The inscription of the charge against him read, “The king of the Jews.” 27And they crucified two outlaws with him, one on his right and one on his left. 29Those who passed by defamed him, shaking their heads and saying, “Aha! You who can destroy the temple and rebuild it in three days, 30save yourself and come down from the cross!” 31In the same way even the chief priests—together with the experts in the law—were mocking him among themselves: “He saved others, but he cannot save himself! 32Let the Christ, the king of Israel, come down from the cross now, that we may see and believe!” Those who were crucified with him also spoke abusively to him.

elements.question: What stands out to you about the way Mark describes Jesus’ death?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Mark’s account is remarkably restrained — he does not dwell on the physical suffering in graphic detail, but the scene he constructs is deeply striking. What stands out is the universal rejection: the soldiers mock him, the crowds mock him, the religious leaders mock him, and even the men crucified alongside him join in. No one defends him. The charge over the cross — “King of the Jews” — is meant as a taunt, but reads as an ironic truth. Jesus absorbs every indignity in silence. And through it all, he stays on the cross; he does not come down to prove himself, even when taunted to do so. Mark presents the crucifixion not as a tragedy that happened to Jesus, but as something he endured deliberately.
elements.questionHint: Look at who is present at the crucifixion and how each group responds — what does the unanimous mockery tell you about how this moment appeared to everyone watching?

elements.text: Jesus is mocked, rejected, and abandoned. Even the two men crucified alongside him join in the taunting. The crowd throws his own words back at him. The religious leaders watch in satisfaction. And those who had followed him most closely are not at the foot of the cross. Outwardly, every marker of success and vindication is absent. This is the picture Mark gives us — deliberately, unflinchingly.

elements.passageText: 33Now when it was noon, darkness came over the whole land until three in the afternoon. 34Around three o’clock Jesus cried out with a loud voice, “Eloi, Eloi, lema sabachthani?” which means, “My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?” 35When some of the bystanders heard it they said, “Listen, he is calling for Elijah!” 36Then someone ran, filled a sponge with sour wine, put it on a stick, and gave it to him to drink, saying, “Leave him alone! Let’s see if Elijah will come to take him down!” 37But Jesus cried out with a loud voice and breathed his last. 38And the temple curtain was torn in two, from top to bottom. 39Now when the centurion, who stood in front of him, saw how he died, he said, “Truly this man was God’s Son!”

elements.question: Why does the cross look like failure? And what does that tell us about how God often works?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: By every visible measure, the cross is catastrophic. The one who claimed authority over wind, sea, sickness, and death is handed over by the people he came to, condemned by a pagan judge, executed as a criminal, and buried in a borrowed tomb. His cry — “My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?” — sounds like abandonment. There is no rescue, no last-minute reversal, no show of power. This tells us something important: God routinely works through means that look, from the outside, like defeat. The cross is not a detour from God’s plan — it is the centre of it. This pattern invites us to reconsider where God might be at work in our own lives in ways we have too quickly dismissed as failure or absence.
elements.questionHint: What two things happen at the moment of Jesus’ death in verses 38–39, and what do they suggest about what God is actually doing at the cross?

elements.text: The Centurion’s Insight

elements.passageText: <sup>39</sup>Now when the centurion, who stood in front of him, saw how he died, he said, “Truly this man was God’s Son!”

elements.question: What is remarkable about who first recognises Jesus’ true identity at the cross?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The centurion is a Roman soldier — a Gentile, a representative of the occupying power that has just executed Jesus, a man with no background in Jewish scripture or messianic expectation. He has no theological stake in who Jesus is. He has just overseen a crucifixion. And yet he is the first human voice in the whole Gospel to clearly declare Jesus ”God’s Son.” Meanwhile, the chief priests, the scribes, and the crowd — all of them steeped in Scripture and awaiting the Messiah — see only a failed pretender. This is a sharp irony: the insider experts are blinded by their own assumptions, while an outsider, watching how Jesus dies, sees the truth clearly.
elements.questionHint: Think about who this man is and what background he comes from — why is it surprising that it is him, rather than any of the religious leaders or disciples, who makes this declaration?

elements.text: When the centurion — a Roman soldier, a pagan, a man with no stake in Jewish messianic hopes — sees how Jesus dies, he says: ”Surely this man was the Son of God!” This is the clearest human declaration of Jesus’ identity in the entire Gospel. And it comes not at a miracle, not at the Transfiguration, but at the moment of greatest apparent defeat. Somehow, the cross itself is what reveals the truth about Jesus. Clarity comes not despite the suffering, but through it.

elements.question: What does the centurion understand? What does it take to see the cross rightly?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Mark tells us the centurion saw ”how he died” — not the miracles, not the teaching, but the manner of his death. Something in the way Jesus faced suffering and death — without bitterness, without calling for rescue, with what appears to be a strange dignity — led the soldier to conclude that this was no ordinary man. To see the cross rightly seems to require letting go of the expectation that power and truth will always look impressive. The religious leaders were looking for a conquering Messiah and so saw only failure. The centurion had no such expectation and therefore could see what was actually there. Seeing the cross rightly requires honesty about our own need, and a willingness to let Jesus be other than we expected him to be.
elements.questionHint: Mark says the centurion responded to ”how” Jesus died — what might that detail suggest about what kind of perception is needed to see the cross for what it really is?

elements.text: The Empty Tomb

elements.question: What is the significance of the women’s fear and astonishment at the tomb?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The women came to the tomb expecting to find a body — they were even discussing who would help them move the stone. The resurrection was not on their horizon; they had come to perform the customary act of anointing a dead man. Their terror and astonishment at finding the tomb empty, and at the angel’s announcement, signals that this event was not what anyone was anticipating or hoping for. It was not the product of wishful thinking. The very people who loved Jesus most were not expecting this. Their overwhelming reaction is Mark’s way of saying: this is not an ordinary event. The resurrection is not a comfort — it is a confrontation with something beyond the categories of ordinary human experience.
elements.questionHint: The women came prepared for one thing and found something completely different — what does their reaction tell you about whether the resurrection was something they were expecting or hoping for?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>When the Sabbath was over, Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James, and Salome bought aromatic spices so that they might go and anoint him. <sup>2</sup>And very early on the first day of the week, at sunrise, they went to the tomb. <sup>3</sup>They had been asking each other, “Who will roll away the stone for us from the entrance to the tomb?” <sup>4</sup>But when they looked up, they saw that the stone, which was very large, had been rolled back. <sup>5</sup>Then as they went into the tomb, they saw a young man dressed in a white robe sitting on the right side; and they were alarmed. <sup>6</sup>But he said to them, “Do not be alarmed. You are looking for Jesus the Nazarene, who was crucified. He has been raised! He is not here. Look, there is the place where they laid him. <sup>7</sup>But go, tell his disciples, even Peter, that he is going ahead of you into Galilee. You will see him there, just as he told you.” <sup>8</sup>Then they went out and ran from the tomb, for terror and bewilderment had seized them. And they said nothing to anyone, because they were afraid.

elements.text: Three women come to the tomb at dawn, expecting to anoint a body. They find the stone rolled away and a young man in white who tells them Jesus has risen and gone ahead of his disciples. Mark’s account ends in a way that feels deliberately unresolved: the women flee, trembling and bewildered. The resurrection is not presented as a tidy, comfortable conclusion. It is presented as something overwhelming — something that demands a response and opens a new chapter.

elements.question: The empty tomb doesn’t just answer questions — it raises them. What questions does it raise for you?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The resurrection raises the question that the whole Gospel has been pressing: if Jesus is truly risen, then who is he — and what does that mean for me? It raises questions about death: if he passed through it and came out the other side, what does that mean for my own death, and for those I have lost? It raises questions about his promises: if he kept this one — the hardest one to believe — what should I make of his other words? And it raises an inescapable question of response: the angel tells the women to go and tell the disciples. The empty tomb is not a fact to file away; it demands a decision about how to live.
elements.questionHint: The angel’s message includes an instruction — what does that tell you about what the resurrection is meant to produce in those who encounter it?

elements.text: Reflection Questions

elements.question: The cross is the clearest revelation of both who Jesus is and how God works. How does it challenge the way you expect God to act in your own life?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: We tend to look for God in success, strength, and growth — not in suffering and apparent failure. Where might God be at work in your life in ways you have been too quick to dismiss?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: The centurion saw something at the cross that the religious experts missed entirely. What made the difference? What does that tell you about what it takes to see Jesus clearly?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: The resurrection is the foundation of the Christian hope. How much does it actually shape the way you live day to day?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.text: Application Challenge

Set aside time this week — longer than your usual daily quiet time — to read and reflect on the crucifixion account in Mark 15. Don’t rush through it. Sit with it. Ask God to help you see the cross clearly, and to let what you see reshape how you live.

elements.question: It’s worth reading the whole passage before you begin...

elements.text:
Mark’s Gospel is bookended by two healings of blind men. The one at the end is dramatic and instant — Bartimaeus calls out, Jesus calls him, and immediately he can see. But the one in the middle of the Gospel, at Bethsaida, is different. Jesus touches the man’s eyes — and the man can see, but only partially. People look like trees, walking around. Jesus touches him again, and then he can see clearly.

Why would Mark include a healing that happens in stages? Jesus could have healed him fully the first time. The answer, surely, is that this is not simply a story about one man’s eyes. It is a picture of the disciples — and of us. Spiritual sight often comes gradually. First we see something. Then, through Jesus’ continued work, we begin to see clearly.
Partial Sight

elements.question: What does ”partial sight” look like in the spiritual life?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The man at Bethsaida after the first touch can see, but what he sees is blurry and misshapen — he mistakes people for walking trees. He is not blind, but he is not seeing clearly either. In the spiritual life, “partial sight” describes the condition of someone who has genuinely encountered Jesus and has some real faith, but whose picture of him is still distorted by assumptions, fears, cultural expectations, or incomplete understanding. They may have correct beliefs at one level while still misunderstanding what Jesus is actually like, what he actually demands, or how he actually works. The disciples throughout Mark are a good example: they believe enough to follow, but repeatedly misread Jesus’ identity, mission, and the nature of discipleship.
elements.questionHint: The man can see something after the first touch, but what he sees is distorted — how might that describe someone who has some genuine faith but not yet a clear picture of Jesus?

elements.passageText: <sup>22</sup>Then they came to Bethsaida. They brought a blind man to Jesus and asked him to touch him. <sup>23</sup>He took the blind man by the hand and brought him outside of the village. Then he spit on his eyes, placed his hands on his eyes, and asked, “Do you see anything?” <sup>24</sup>The man looked up and said, “I see people, but they look like trees walking.”

elements.text: Partial sight is not the same as blindness. The man at Bethsaida can see something — he is not in total darkness. But what he sees is blurry and distorted. He mistakes people for trees. He is moving, but not toward an accurate picture of reality. This is a helpful picture of where many of us spend much of our Christian life. We have seen something of Jesus — enough to follow him. But our picture is still incomplete, still shaped by our assumptions, our fears, our cultural blind spots.

elements.question: Where in your understanding of Jesus do you think you might still be seeing ”people like trees”?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: This is an honest self-examination question. Areas where distorted vision is common might include: seeing Jesus primarily as a moral teacher while underestimating his authority and Lordship; expecting him to work through success and strength while struggling to accept the pattern of the cross; treating him as a resource for a better life rather than the one who makes an absolute claim on my whole existence; or inheriting a culturally shaped picture of Jesus that reflects my community’s preferences more than the Jesus of the Gospels. The passage itself suggests that the problem is not a lack of faith, but faith that has not yet been fully clarified — a starting point, not a permanent condition.
elements.questionHint: Think about the ways the disciples in Mark kept misunderstanding Jesus — which of their misunderstandings do you find yourself most identifying with?

elements.text: Clear Sight

elements.passageText: <sup>25</sup>Then Jesus placed his hands on the man’s eyes again, and the man looked intently and his sight was restored — he saw everything clearly. <sup>26</sup>Jesus sent him home, saying, “Do not even go into the village.”

elements.question: What changes between the two stages of the healing?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The only thing that changes is that Jesus touches him again. The man does not do anything differently — he does not try harder, concentrate more, or achieve clarity through his own effort. Jesus takes the initiative a second time, and then the man sees clearly. This is the central point of the two-stage healing as a picture of spiritual growth: clarity of vision is not the result of the believer’s increasing effort or insight. It is the result of Jesus’ continued, gracious work. We do not see him more clearly by straining harder; we are brought to see him more clearly as he continues to work in us through his word, his Spirit, and the community of faith.
elements.questionHint: What does the man do to move from partial sight to clear sight — and what does that tell you about where the power for spiritual growth actually comes from?

elements.text: Jesus touches him again. That’s what changes. The man does not try harder to see. He does not achieve clarity through effort or insight. Jesus does something more. And then the man sees clearly. This is the pattern throughout Mark’s Gospel. Clarity about Jesus is not primarily the result of intellectual effort — it is the result of Jesus’ ongoing work in us. We are not working our way toward a clear picture of him; he is working to give us clearer sight.

elements.question: What does this teach us about how we should pray, and what we should pray for?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The healing teaches us to pray with dependence rather than self-sufficiency. Since clarity of sight comes from Jesus’ work rather than our own, the right posture is to ask him for it — genuinely, persistently, and with the expectation that he is willing and able to give it. We should pray specifically for clearer sight: to see Jesus more truly, to have our distorted images corrected, and to understand his word and his ways more deeply. The blind man in the story did not produce his own healing; he received it. Prayer for spiritual growth should reflect that same posture — not ”help me try harder” but ”do in me what only you can do.”
elements.questionHint: If the man couldn’t bring about his own clear sight — if it required Jesus to act — what does that suggest about the right way to approach our own spiritual growth?

elements.text: Looking Back Across the Gospel

As you come to the end of these studies, it is worth stepping back and tracing the thread that runs through Mark.

Jesus’ identity is constantly misunderstood — and then finally clarified, not by a disciple’s great insight, but by a Roman soldier at the foot of the cross. His mission is constantly resisted — even by his closest friends — and then fulfilled through suffering. His call to discipleship is repeatedly avoided and redefined — and the cross shows what it truly costs.

The disciples do not arrive at clarity on their own. They are brought to it, slowly, through continued exposure to Jesus — his words, his actions, and ultimately his death and resurrection. The same is true for us.
Reflection Questions

elements.question: What have these studies shown you about Jesus that you had not seen clearly before?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection...

elements.question: Reflecting on the whole of Mark’s Gospel, where has your understanding of Jesus changed or deepened during these studies?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: Where do you think you are still seeing only partially — still letting assumptions, expectations, or fears blur your picture of who Jesus is?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: The disciples were not left in their partial sight. Jesus continued to work in them. How does that give you hope for your own growth?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: What would it look like, concretely, to keep exposing yourself to Jesus — to keep putting yourself in the place where his continued work can bring greater clarity?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.text: Application Challenge

Make a commitment about how you will continue to grow in seeing Jesus clearly after these studies end. It might be a regular pattern of Bible reading, a group you will join, a person you will study Scripture with. Write it down and share it with someone who can encourage you.
Final Prayer

Lord Jesus, we often see you only partially. Our expectations, our fears, and our sin distort the picture. Open our eyes. Help us to see who you really are — not the Jesus we have constructed, but the Jesus who arrives as King, who calls us to follow at great cost, who goes to the cross in our place, and who rises with authority over death itself. Remove our blindness, do your work in us, and lead us to trust you fully. Amen.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Introducing Mark
studyOnboardingSlides.body:
A eight-session Bible study on the book of Mark.

Work through each chapter at your own pace, or use it with a group.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Read, reflect, write
studyOnboardingSlides.body: Each chapter has Bible questions tied to specific passages, and personal reflection questions at the end. Tap ⓘ beside any Bible reference to read the passage without leaving the app.

studyOnboardingSlides.body: Your answers are saved automatically as you type, and stored on this device. Use the Share button at the bottom of each chapter to send your answers to a group leader or mentor.
