studyOnboardingSlides.body: If you need bigger text go straight to settings now
studyOnboardingSlides.bodyAfter: Tap the ⚙ icon in the top corner to open Settings any time to change text size or switch to dark mode. Tap ☰ (contents) to jump to any chapter.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: How to use this app
studyOnboardingSlides.body: If you want a full guide to getting the most from this app and this study – whether on your own or in a group – you can tap the button below.

action.label: Go to the ‘How to use’ page →

studyOnboardingSlides.bodyAfter: You can also find it any time via the ? icon in the top navigation bar.

blocks.title: People who are curious about Jesus
blocks.body: Mark’s Gospel is one of the best places to start if you want to encounter Jesus directly, without much prior knowledge. This study doesn’t assume you are already a Christian or that you have any background in the Bible. It simply takes you through what Mark wrote and invites you to look carefully and decide what you make of it.

blocks.title: People who think they already know the story
blocks.body: One of the striking things about Mark is how often the people closest to Jesus completely misread him — even after months of travelling with him. If you have grown up in church and feel you know who Jesus is, these studies are designed to press past familiarity and ask whether you are seeing him clearly, or whether your picture of him has been quietly shaped by assumptions and expectations he doesn’t actually fit.

blocks.title: Groups and discipleship contexts
blocks.body: The eight sessions cover the main movements of Mark’s Gospel and work well in a small group, a one-to-one discipleship setting, or as preparation for baptism or membership. Each chapter is self-contained but builds on what has come before, so working through them in order is worthwhile.

blocks.title: On your own
blocks.body: This study works well as a personal study. Read the Bible passage at the start of each chapter before you begin — the study is designed around Mark’s text, and the questions will make much more sense if you have the passage fresh in your mind. Then work through the questions at your own pace, writing your answers as you go. The reflection questions at the end are worth sitting with before moving on.

blocks.title: In a group
blocks.body: The study works well in a small group setting. The most effective approach is usually for each person to work through the chapter individually first — reading the passage and writing their answers — and then for the group to come together to compare what they found. This means the discussion starts from genuine engagement with the text rather than first impressions. Mark raises questions that reward honest conversation.

blocks.title: Reading Mark alongside the study
blocks.body: Each chapter covers a specific section of Mark, and the study is not a substitute for reading the Gospel itself. If you want to get the most from these sessions, consider reading the whole of Mark’s Gospel at a single sitting before you begin — it takes about two hours. Seeing the whole story first makes each individual chapter richer.

blocks.title: Working with a leader or mentor
blocks.body:
If someone has recommended this study to you — a pastor, a small group leader, a friend who wants to work through it with you — that relationship is one of the most valuable things you can bring to it. The questions Mark raises about who Jesus is and what following him costs are not easy ones, and having someone to think them through with makes a real difference.

At the end of each chapter, the Share button lets you send your written answers directly to your leader or mentor — via WhatsApp, email, or any other app on your phone. This gives them a window into your thinking before you meet, and makes for a much richer conversation.

blocks.title: Two kinds of questions
blocks.body: Each chapter has two kinds of questions. The Bible questions follow the structure of the passage — asking you to look carefully at specific details of what Mark records: what Jesus says, what he does, how people respond, and what that reveals about who he is. The reflection questions at the end turn the same material toward you personally — asking what you make of Jesus, and what his claims demand of you.

blocks.title: How to read the passages
blocks.body: Each section of the study begins with a prompt to read a specific passage from Mark. Don’t skip this — the questions are designed to reward close attention to the text itself. Tap the ↗ arrow beside any Bible reference to read the passage in a pop-up without leaving the app. The translation used throughout is the NET Bible.

blocks.title: The thread running through Mark
blocks.body: A single question runs through all eight sessions: Who is Jesus — and are we seeing him clearly? The disciples, the crowds, and the religious leaders all struggle with this in different ways. As you work through each chapter, notice how the misunderstanding accumulates — and how Mark steadily brings the reader to a clearer and more demanding picture of who Jesus actually is.

intro: These notes are written for anyone leading a group through Introducing Mark — whether you are an experienced Bible teacher or someone stepping into this role for the first time. They are not a script. They are a preparation tool: background to help you understand the passage more deeply, pastoral guidance to help you read your group, and warnings about the places where discussion tends to go sideways.

Mark’s Gospel is the shortest and most urgent of the four. Where Matthew and Luke provide birth narratives and Luke gives us an extended prologue, Mark simply begins: ”The beginning of the good news about Jesus the Messiah, the Son of God.” His word for ”immediately” (euthys) appears over forty times. Things happen fast. There is an almost breathless quality to the narrative, as if Mark wants to press the reader forward before they have time to settle into a comfortable distance from what Jesus is doing and saying.

That urgency should shape how you lead. The goal of these sessions is not primarily to transfer information about Mark’s Gospel. It is to bring people into genuine encounter with the Jesus Mark is presenting — and to keep pressing the question the Gospel itself keeps pressing: Who is this man, and what will you do with him? Everything else — the background, the exegesis, the discussion — is in service of that.

A note on the text. Mark’s Greek is plain and direct — almost colloquial in places. He uses the present tense to narrate past events (a device called the historical present), which gives the narrative an eyewitness, you-are-there quality. Many scholars believe Peter’s preaching lies behind the Gospel, which would explain both the vividness of detail and the occasionally unflattering portrayal of the disciples. When the text feels blunt or urgent, that is a feature, not a flaw — let it be.

A note on the study structure. Each chapter in this study covers a substantial passage of Mark and is built around a central question or theme. The study questions are designed to be worked through by individuals before the group meets — they are not cold-open discussion prompts. Your job in the group is not to get through all the questions but to go deeper into the ones that matter most for your particular group. Pay attention to where energy and honesty emerge, and follow that.

A note on leading. The most important thing you can do as a leader is not to have all the answers. It is to model genuine engagement with the text — asking real questions, sitting with difficulty, being willing to say ’I find that hard too.’ Groups take their cue from their leader. A leader who approaches the text with intellectual curiosity and honest faith will give others permission to do the same.

background.body:
Mark 1:1–15 is a compressed prologue that does the work Matthew and Luke distribute across two or three chapters. Three scenes follow in rapid succession: John the Baptist preparing the way (vv1–8), the baptism of Jesus (vv9–11), and the temptation in the wilderness (vv12–13). The whole unit is bracketed by the summary of Jesus’ preaching in vv14–15, which functions as the thesis statement for everything that follows.

The opening verse is deliberately allusive. ”The beginning of the good news” echoes the opening words of Genesis (“In the beginning...”) and signals that what follows is a new creation event. ”Jesus Christ” combines a personal name with a title: Christos is the Greek equivalent of the Hebrew Mashiach (Messiah), meaning ”anointed one.” ”Son of God” appears in some early manuscripts and not others — the textual question is genuinely contested — but fits Mark’s overall purpose of establishing Jesus’ divine identity from the outset.

The composite quotation in vv2–3 is attributed to Isaiah but draws on both Exodus 23:20/Malachi 3:1 and Isaiah 40:3. Mark’s attribution of the combined quotation to Isaiah alone is sometimes pointed to as an error; it is more likely a deliberate theological decision to frame the whole under Isaiah’s restoration theology, with the Malachi material seen as part of the same prophetic tradition. The key point is that Isaiah 40 describes the return of God himself to his people — and Mark applies that to the arrival of Jesus.

The baptism scene (vv9–11) is a Trinitarian moment: the voice of the Father speaks over the Son as the Spirit descends. The heavens ”tearing apart” (Greek: schizō) is a violent image — the same word is used for the temple curtain tearing at the crucifixion (15:38), creating a literary bracket around the whole Gospel. The declaration ”You are my beloved Son” combines Psalm 2:7 (the royal coronation psalm) and Isaiah 42:1 (the Servant of the Lord), identifying Jesus as both King and Servant.

The temptation account (vv12–13) is strikingly brief compared to Matthew and Luke. Mark gives no details of the specific temptations, only that Jesus ”endured” them. The forty days recall Israel’s forty years in the wilderness, and the angels ministering to Jesus may echo Elijah in 1 Kings 19. The point is typological: where Israel failed, Jesus succeeds.

keyPoints.body:
This chapter establishes the identity of Jesus before a single miracle is performed or a single sermon preached. Mark is not building gradually toward a reveal — he puts the conclusion in the first sentence and then spends the rest of the Gospel showing us what it means and why it is hard to grasp.

Help your group feel the weight of the two titles in v1. ”Messiah” was not a vague spiritual term — it carried specific political and national expectations. ”Son of God” goes further still. Mark is not hedging; he is making the maximum possible claim from line one.

The ”good news” language (v1, v14) is worth dwelling on. In the Roman world, euangelion was used for imperial announcements — the birth of an heir, a military victory. Mark is deliberately co-opting the language of Caesar’s propaganda and applying it to Jesus. There is a quietly subversive quality to the opening of this Gospel that your group may find surprising.

The pairing of repentance and faith in v15 is the hinge on which everything in the study turns. Make sure the group understands these not as one-time events but as an ongoing orientation — a posture of ongoing turning and ongoing trust.

pastorals.body:
Some in your group may have heard the opening of Mark many times and will be tempted to settle quickly into familiarity. Resist the rush. Mark wants to disorient as well as inform — the compressed prologue is meant to be striking. Ask people what they actually notice in the text, rather than what they already knew was there.

The baptism scene raises the question of Jesus submitting to a baptism ”for the forgiveness of sins.” If someone asks why the sinless Jesus was baptised, don’t deflect: it is a good question. Mark doesn’t explain it directly. Matthew does (3:15 — “to fulfil all righteousness”). The most pastoral answer is that Jesus enters fully into the human condition, including our place of repentance — not because he needs it, but as the first act of identification with those he has come to save.

The temptation account may raise questions about whether Jesus could actually have sinned. This is the classic theological debate between peccability and impeccability. It is worth acknowledging the question without getting drawn into an extended detour. The pastoral point is simpler: Jesus was genuinely tested, not theatrically. His faithfulness is real, not guaranteed by a kind of divine safety net that made the testing meaningless.

Some people in your group may find the command to ”repent” heavy or shame-inducing. Help them hear it as John’s hearers did — not as condemnation but as the realistic and necessary first step toward something wonderful. The kingdom is near; repentance is how you receive it, not what you pay for it.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the temptation to turn this chapter into a survey of background knowledge rather than a genuine encounter with the text. Resist providing too much information too quickly. The goal is not that people leave knowing more about first-century Jewish messianic expectations — it is that they feel the force of Mark’s opening claim about Jesus and take it seriously.

background.body:
Mark 1:16–2:12 covers a dense sequence of events: the call of the first disciples (1:16–20), the exorcism in the Capernaum synagogue (1:21–28), the healing of Simon’s mother-in-law (1:29–31), the evening healings (1:32–34), Jesus withdrawing to pray (1:35–39), and the healing of a leper (1:40–45), culminating in the healing of the paralysed man and the forgiveness controversy (2:1–12).

The call of the first disciples (1:16–20) is deliberately abrupt. Mark gives no prior relationship between Jesus and these men — no context for their willingness to follow. This is almost certainly a narrative decision: Mark wants the reader to feel the sheer authority in the call rather than explain it away by a prior acquaintance (John’s Gospel, by contrast, gives us the backstory in John 1:35–42). The ”immediacy” emphasis (Greek: euthys) is characteristic of Mark’s style throughout.

The exorcism in the synagogue (1:21–28) is the first miracle in the Gospel. The unclean spirit’s cry — “I know who you are — the Holy One of God!” — is theologically loaded. Demons recognise Jesus where humans fail to. This is one of the great ironies of Mark’s Gospel: those who should recognise Jesus (religious leaders, family) don’t; those who have no stake in it (Roman soldiers, unclean spirits) do.

The healing of the paralysed man (2:1–12) is the theological climax of this section. The controversy it generates is the first direct conflict between Jesus and the religious establishment. The scribes’ question in v7 — “Who can forgive sins but God alone?” — is exegetically correct. Their error is not in their theology of forgiveness but in their Christology. Jesus’ claim is not that he is acting on God’s behalf (like a prophet) but that he himself has the authority that belongs to God.

The title ”Son of Man” (v10) is Jesus’ most characteristic self-designation in Mark and is drawn from Daniel 7:13–14, where the Son of Man is given authority, glory, and sovereign power. It is both a human and a heavenly figure — an ambiguous title that is richer than either ”Messiah” or ”Son of God” taken alone.

keyPoints.body:
The theme running through this chapter is authority — specifically, the kind of authority that is demonstrated rather than merely claimed. Jesus teaches with authority (1:22), commands unclean spirits with authority (1:27), and claims authority to forgive sins (2:10) — and in each case, the authority is backed by action. Make sure your group sees that Mark is not presenting Jesus as a great teacher with a compelling message; he is presenting someone who has power over the spiritual realm, the physical body, and the moral order.

The distinction the crowd makes in 1:22 — that Jesus teaches ”not like the experts in the law” — is worth unpacking. The scribes derived their authority by citation: Rabbi X says this, Rabbi Y says that. Jesus speaks in his own name, with a first-person directness that belongs to no one else in the room. This is not just a different teaching style; it is an implicit claim to a different kind of authority altogether.

The paralysed man passage (2:1–12) is the hinge of the chapter. Press the group to engage with the scribes’ logic: they are actually correct that only God can forgive sins. That is precisely the point. Jesus is not claiming prophetic authority (speaking on God’s behalf); he is claiming divine authority (acting as God). The healing is offered as proof — not proof that Jesus is a good man, but proof that his claim about forgiveness is backed by real power.

pastorals.body:
The immediacy of the disciples’ response to Jesus’ call (1:18, 20) can feel unrealistic to modern readers, and some in your group may resist it as such. Acknowledge this honestly — Mark is not giving us a psychological explanation, he is showing us the weight of the call. The question worth pressing is not ”why did they respond so quickly?” but ”what does their response cost them, and what does that cost tell us about what Jesus expects?”

Some group members may be troubled by the idea that Jesus forgives sins — specifically by the question of whether he can forgive sins committed against other people. This is a legitimate philosophical point (raised famously by C.S. Lewis). The answer is that if Jesus is who he claims to be, then all sin is ultimately against God, and the one who is God has the authority to forgive it all. Spend time here only if it arises naturally; don’t plant the objection.

The four friends who lower the paralysed man through the roof (2:3–4) often generate warm discussion about bringing people to Jesus. Be careful that this doesn’t become a guilt-inducing conversation about evangelistic failure. The passage is primarily about what Jesus does when faith is present — use it to generate hope rather than condemnation.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the group to treat amazement as the endpoint. The crowd’s amazement in 1:22, 1:27, and 2:12 is consistent throughout Mark — and consistently insufficient. Being amazed by Jesus is not the same as submitting to him. Keep pressing the distinction between finding Jesus impressive and genuinely reckoning with his authority over your own life.

background.body:
Mark 2:13–3:35 brings together a series of conflict episodes that escalate steadily in intensity: eating with tax collectors (2:13–17), the question about fasting (2:18–22), two Sabbath controversies (2:23–28; 3:1–6), and the accusation that Jesus is empowered by Beelzebul (3:20–35). By the end of 3:6, the Pharisees are already plotting to kill him — an astonishing development that comes very early in the Gospel.

The old/new wineskins saying (2:21–22) is one of Mark’s most exegetically important passages in this section. Jesus is not simply defending his practice of eating with sinners or his disciples’ failure to fast; he is making a categorical claim about the nature of what he has brought. The old order — Torah observance as the Pharisees understood it — cannot simply be patched or topped up; it must give way to something qualitatively new. This is not anti-Judaism; it is a claim about eschatological newness. The ”new wine” is the kingdom of God breaking in, and the ”old wineskins” represent a religious framework that has become incapable of containing it.

The Sabbath controversies (2:23–3:6) are best understood together. In the grain field, Jesus defends his disciples by citing David eating the showbread (1 Samuel 21:1–6) — arguing from Scripture that human need can override ritual law, and ending with the sweeping claim ”The Son of Man is Lord even of the Sabbath” (2:28). In the synagogue (3:1–6), he frames the issue as a moral question: “Is it lawful to do good or to do evil on the Sabbath?” The silence of the Pharisees is not neutral — it reveals that their concern is not with the law’s purpose (doing good) but with their own authority.

The Beelzebul controversy (3:22–30) is theologically significant beyond the immediate conflict. The ”unforgivable sin” of blasphemy against the Holy Spirit (3:29) has caused enormous anxiety across church history. In context, it refers specifically to the act of attributing the Spirit’s work to Satan — calling good evil, and divine power demonic. It is not a general category of sin that believers might accidentally commit; it is a sustained, deliberate, knowing rejection of what is clearly God’s work. Reassure any group member who is anxious about this: the very fact of their anxiety is evidence they have not committed it.

Jesus’ redefinition of family (3:31–35) is deliberately shocking in a culture where family obligation was among the most binding social forces. He is not dismissing his biological family; he is relativising all human bonds under the claim that doing God’s will constitutes a more fundamental form of belonging.

keyPoints.body:
The most important thing to draw out in this chapter is the irony at the heart of the conflict: the people opposing Jesus are not irreligious. They are the most religiously serious people in the story — and that is precisely what has made them blind. Their framework for understanding what God would do and how he would act has become so fixed that they cannot recognise him when he arrives. This is a deeply unsettling observation, and worth sitting with.

Help your group see the internal logic of the Pharisees’ position. They are not simply being obtuse — they have a coherent theological framework. Their error is not stupidity but a category failure: they are trying to evaluate something genuinely new with criteria developed for something old. The question worth pressing is what the equivalent category failure might look like in your group’s own experience of faith.

Jesus’ anger in 3:5 is important. He is ”grieved by the hardness of their hearts” — a phrase that implies not irritation but a deep, sorrowful indignation. This is not Jesus losing his temper; it is Jesus’ response to people who are watching grace at work and resisting it. It models for your group that it is possible to be angry about the right things in the right way.

pastorals.body:
This chapter can be deeply uncomfortable for people who are themselves committed, thoughtful Christians. The message that religious seriousness can become a barrier to recognising God is threatening rather than reassuring for the conscientious believer. Acknowledge this directly. The antidote is not less conviction but more humility — a willingness to let God’s word judge our frameworks rather than assuming our frameworks correctly judge God.

The ”unforgivable sin” passage almost always generates anxiety in a group — either someone worries they have committed it, or they know someone who does. Be ready with a clear, calm answer: the context is the deliberate, sustained attribution of Jesus’ work to Satan by people who knew better. The person who is anxious about whether they have committed it almost certainly has not; the very capacity for that anxiety reflects a conscience that is still responsive to the Spirit.

Be pastorally attentive to anyone in your group who has had a painful experience with religious authority — being told by a church or leader that something God was doing in their life was wrong or dangerous. This passage speaks directly to that experience, and it can unlock significant pain. Don’t rush past it.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the discussion to become a comfortable critique of the Pharisees — “we would never be like them.” The entire point of the chapter is that the people who resisted Jesus most seriously were the ones who were most confident they were serving God. The question is not ”why were the Pharisees so blind?” but ”where might we be doing the same thing?” Keep pressing the group to apply the observation rather than merely acknowledge it.

background.body:
Mark 4:1–34 is one of the few extended teaching sections in the Gospel — Mark characteristically prefers action to discourse. The chapter contains the parable of the sower with its interpretation (4:1–20), the lamp-under-a-bowl saying (4:21–25), the parable of the growing seed (4:26–29), and the parable of the mustard seed (4:30–32).

The parable of the sower is the foundational parable — Jesus himself says as much in 4:13. If you don’t understand this one, you won’t understand any of them. Its structure is simple: a sower scatters seed, and four different soils produce four different outcomes. The key exegetical point is that the seed is identical in every case. The variable is entirely the soil — that is, the condition of the hearer.

The ”parable theory” passage (4:10–12) is the most exegetically contested in the chapter. Jesus seems to be saying that parables are designed to prevent understanding rather than aid it — a reading that has troubled interpreters for centuries. The quotation in v12 is from Isaiah 6:9–10, where God commissions Isaiah to speak to a people who will hear without understanding — not as the purpose of the message but as its anticipated result. Mark’s use of hina (“so that”) in v12 is grammatically purposive but may reflect a Semitic causal idiom. The most defensible reading is not that Jesus deliberately obscured truth from outsiders, but that parables function as a kind of test: those who press in (as the disciples do in v10) receive the explanation; those who remain passive or resistant remain at the surface. The parables reveal the condition of the listener as much as they communicate truth about the kingdom.

The parable of the growing seed (4:26–29) is unique to Mark. Its emphasis is entirely on the farmer’s passivity — he ”does not know how” the seed grows. This is a significant counter to activist approaches to kingdom work: the growth is God’s, not ours. The farmer sows and harvests; the intervening work belongs to the soil.

The mustard seed (4:30–32) is not claiming the mustard plant is literally the largest plant in the world — it isn’t. Jesus is using hyperbole to make the point about disproportionate growth from small beginnings. The image of birds nesting in the branches may echo Ezekiel 17:23 and Daniel 4:12, where a great tree that shelters the nations is a symbol of a universal kingdom.

keyPoints.body:
The central diagnostic of this chapter is the question of what kind of soil we are. Press the group to be honest rather than aspirational. Most of us are not straightforwardly good soil or straightforwardly hard ground — we are mixtures, and the mixture changes depending on the season and the circumstances. The thorny soil is probably the most recognisable for comfortable, busy people: the word is there, growth begins, but competing concerns gradually crowd it out without any dramatic moment of rejection.

The ”why parables?” section (4:10–12) deserves careful handling. The key insight is that the disciples receive the explanation because they ask — they press in. The posture of genuine enquiry is what makes the difference. This applies directly to how your group members approach God’s word: those who come with genuine hunger, willing to be challenged and changed, are in a position to understand; those who come looking for confirmation of what they already think will remain at the surface.

The two kingdom-growth parables at the end of the chapter are profoundly encouraging and should be allowed to land as such. The kingdom is growing by a power that is not ours, from beginnings that look insignificant, toward an end that is out of all proportion to the start. This is not permission for passivity — the sower still sows — but it is freedom from the burden of feeling that the outcome depends on us.

pastorals.body:
The parable of the sower can be anxiety-inducing for people who are not sure which soil describes them, or who worry that they might be the rocky ground (initially enthusiastic but without depth). Be careful not to resolve this anxiety too quickly — some honest self-examination is the point. But also make clear that the parables are not diagnostic tools for permanent categories; they are invitations to examine the present condition of our hearts and to ask Jesus to change what we find there.

People who have been Christians for a long time sometimes find the ”what kind of soil are you?” question either too simple or too threatening. Encourage them to think about it not as a general life question but in relation to specific things: specific areas of obedience, specific ways God’s word has been crowded out, specific forms of growth that have stalled. The more specific the self-examination, the more useful it is.

The parable theory (4:11–12) can feel unfair — as if God is deliberately keeping some people in the dark. Acknowledge this honestly. The best pastoral response is to invite the group to notice what the disciples did: they asked. That is always available to anyone. The question is not whether understanding is available, but whether we want it enough to pursue it.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the group to identify comfortably with the good soil and locate the other soils in people they know. The parable is a mirror, not a window. Keep bringing the application back to the individual: which of these obstacles do you most recognise in your own life right now?

background.body:
Mark 4:35–6:6 contains some of the most vivid narrative material in the Gospel: the stilling of the storm (4:35–41), the healing of the Gerasene demoniac (5:1–20), the raising of Jairus’s daughter interleaved with the healing of the bleeding woman (5:21–43), and the rejection at Nazareth (6:1–6).

The stilling of the storm (4:35–41) is unmistakeably an echo of Old Testament theophanies — particularly Psalm 107:23–32, where God stills the storm and brings sailors safely to port. By speaking to the wind and sea, Jesus does what Yahweh does. The disciples’ question — “Who then is this?” — is the right question, and Mark leaves it deliberately unanswered at this point.

The Gerasene demoniac (5:1–20) is the most dramatic exorcism account in the Gospels. Several details reward attention: the name ”Legion” (a Roman military unit of around 6,000 men) may carry a political resonance — the power occupying this man has a name that evokes the occupying power of Rome. The pigs are unclean animals, making the Decapolis region Gentile territory, which means Jesus is crossing a significant boundary. The man is told, uniquely among those Jesus heals, to go and tell what the Lord has done for him — because in Gentile territory there is no risk of a premature messianic expectation (the so-called ”messianic secret” is not operative here).

The interleaved stories of Jairus and the bleeding woman (5:21–43) is a Markan literary device called intercalation or ”sandwiching” — he inserts one story inside another to invite comparison. Both involve a woman/girl at the point of death; both involve a twelve-year connection (the girl is twelve, the woman has suffered twelve years); both result in restoration. The bleeding woman’s condition made her ritually unclean under Levitical law, making her contact with Jesus an act of considerable social and religious transgression. Jesus’ response — he calls her ”Daughter” and commends her faith — is remarkable in its gentleness and dignity.

The rejection at Nazareth (6:1–6) contains one of the most surprising verses in the Gospel: “He was not able to do many miracles there” (v5). This does not imply a limitation in Jesus’ power; it reflects the nature of miracles in Mark’s understanding — they occur in the context of faith and trust, not as performances to compel belief. The townspeople’s familiarity with Jesus functions as a wall, not a window.

keyPoints.body:
This chapter presents three demonstrations of Jesus’ comprehensive authority — over creation (the storm), over spiritual powers (Legion), over illness and death (the bleeding woman and Jairus’s daughter) — and then, immediately, shows that familiarity can neutralise the impact of even the greatest evidence. The structure is deliberate: Mark wants us to feel the incongruity of the Nazareth rejection against the backdrop of what Jesus has just done.

The storm passage should not be allowed to become purely an allegory (“Jesus calms the storms of our lives”). The disciples’ panic is the real subject, and Jesus’ question — “Do you still not have faith?” — is the real challenge. The word ”still” implies accumulated evidence that should by now have produced trust. Press the group to sit with the reproach rather than move quickly to reassurance.

The Nazareth passage is pastorally important because it names a real spiritual danger: familiarity. People who have grown up in the church, or who have been Christians for decades, are not immune to the pattern the townspeople embody — knowing enough about Jesus to feel they are not surprised by anything he says or does, and therefore never being genuinely confronted by him. This is a form of spiritual insulation, not faith.

pastorals.body:
The storm passage often surfaces real fears in a group. Some people will have gone through experiences where God seemed absent — a crisis in which they prayed and nothing changed, where the storm got worse rather than better. Be honest about this. Jesus’ rebuke of the disciples is not a template for rebuking Christians who struggle in suffering. The disciples had Jesus physically present and still panicked; the rebuke addresses a specific failure in that specific context. The more general pastoral point is gentler: are we bringing our fears to Jesus, or are we handling them alone?

The bleeding woman (5:25–34) is a passage about desperation and courage. She has spent everything on medical help with no result, and her condition places her outside normal social and religious life. Her action in touching Jesus’ cloak is both an act of faith and an act of transgression. Jesus’ response publicly restores her — not just physically, but socially and relationally. This is worth dwelling on for anyone in your group whose experience of suffering has involved isolation or shame.

The Nazareth rejection may be painful for those who have experienced rejection from their own family or community because of faith. The passage doesn’t explain or resolve it — Jesus simply notes it and moves on. Sometimes that is the most honest pastoral response: acknowledging the reality without offering a tidy explanation.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the group to locate the ”familiarity” problem safely in other people. Many people who are regular churchgoers are more vulnerable to the Nazareth dynamic than they recognise — the language of faith can become so familiar that it no longer carries its actual weight. Ask the group directly: Is there anything Jesus says in Mark that genuinely surprises you anymore — and if not, what does that tell you?

background.body:
Mark 8:27–9:1 is widely recognised as the structural hinge of the Gospel. The confession at Caesarea Philippi (8:27–30) marks the point at which the question of Jesus’ identity receives its first clear human answer — and immediately raises the question of what kind of Messiah he is. The three sub-sections that follow (8:31–33, 8:34–38, 9:1) constitute a concentrated teaching on suffering, discipleship, and the coming kingdom.

Caesarea Philippi is a significant geographical choice. It was a city built by Philip the Tetrarch to honour Caesar Augustus, containing a prominent temple to the god Pan and a shrine to Caesar. This is the most politically loaded location in the region — and it is here that Jesus asks who people say he is, and who the disciples say he is. The implicit contrast with the claims of Caesar is probably deliberate.

Peter’s confession — “You are the Christ” — is the climax of the first half of the Gospel. It is the correct answer. But Mark immediately shows that getting the title right is not the same as understanding what it means. Peter’s rebuke of Jesus (8:32) reveals that his concept of Messiah is shaped by a theology of triumph rather than suffering. Jesus’ counter-rebuke — “Get behind me, Satan!” — is one of the harshest things he says to a disciple in the Gospel. The word ”Satan” means ”adversary” — Peter, in trying to redirect Jesus away from the cross, is functionally doing what Satan does: opposing the mission of God.

The first passion prediction (8:31) is the first of three in Mark (8:31; 9:31; 10:33–34). Each is progressively more detailed. After each prediction, the disciples fail in some way that illustrates their continued misunderstanding. After the first, Peter rebukes Jesus (8:32). After the second, the disciples argue about who is greatest (9:34). After the third, James and John ask for the best seats (10:35–37). Mark’s irony is devastating.

The discipleship saying (8:34–38) is addressed to ”the crowd along with his disciples” — it is not an esoteric teaching for insiders but a public announcement. The cross was not a religious symbol in the first century; it was an instrument of state execution. The image of taking up one’s cross would have been understood immediately as walking to your death. The paradox of losing your life to save it (8:35) is the central paradox of Christian discipleship.

The phrase ”in this adulterous and sinful generation” (8:38) echoes Old Testament language for Israel’s unfaithfulness to God — being ”ashamed of” Jesus and his words is a form of the same betrayal.

keyPoints.body:
The central pastoral challenge of this chapter is to press the group past Peter’s correct answer to the much harder question of what that answer implies for how they live. Knowing Jesus is the Christ is not enough — Peter knew that and immediately tried to stop Jesus going to the cross. The question is: do we understand what kind of Messiah he is, and does that understanding shape our expectations of God and our response to suffering?

The contrast between Peter’s human thinking and God’s thinking (8:33) is worth dwelling on. Peter’s error is not stupidity or wickedness — it is a very natural assumption that power, success, and the avoidance of suffering are what God’s favour looks like. Jesus inverts this entirely. The pattern of the cross — that God’s greatest work is accomplished through what looks like defeat — is one that most of us find as hard to accept as Peter did.

The discipleship teaching (8:34–38) should not be softened. ”Take up your cross” is not a metaphor for minor inconvenience — it is a call to lay down the right to manage your own life. Press the group to name what that means specifically, rather than allowing it to remain at the level of a general principle.

pastorals.body:
People who have suffered significantly — illness, bereavement, persecution, failure — may find this chapter either very liberating or very difficult. For those who have been told (explicitly or implicitly) that suffering means God’s absence or displeasure, the message that the cross is the centre of God’s plan rather than a deviation from it can be profoundly releasing. For those who are currently in acute pain, the call to ”take up your cross” can feel like being asked to embrace what is destroying them. Be sensitive to the difference.

The ”lose your life to save it” paradox (8:35) often generates the question: what does this mean practically? Resist the temptation to give too neat an answer. The most honest pastoral response is that it looks different for different people in different seasons, and that the common element is a decision about who is directing your life — not a single act of surrender but an ongoing orientation.

Some in your group may be in relationships or careers where following Jesus has an immediate and concrete cost — relationships with family who oppose their faith, professional pressures to compromise, social costs of standing for something. This passage speaks directly to them. Be ready to pray specifically for courage rather than simply affirming the general principle.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the group to domesticate the cross-bearing saying into something manageable — “my cross is my difficult colleague” or ”my cross is my health condition.” These things are real hardships, and Jesus does not dismiss them. But ”take up your cross” in context means something more specific: the deliberate, conscious subordination of your own agenda to Jesus’s. Keep that edge in the conversation.

chapters.title: The Cross and the Empty Tomb

background.body:
Mark 15–16 brings the Gospel to its conclusion. The passion narrative in Mark is spare and controlled — he does not dwell on the physical details of crucifixion as later Christian piety sometimes has. What he focuses on instead is the theological and dramatic dimensions: the mockery that is ironically true, the darkness over the land, the cry of dereliction, the tearing of the temple curtain, and the centurion’s confession.

Pilate’s role is presented ambivalently. He appears to know Jesus is innocent (15:10 — he knows the chief priests acted out of envy; 15:14 — he asks ”what crime has he committed?”) but capitulates to crowd pressure. The release of Barabbas (15:6–15) is a vivid illustration of substitution: a guilty man goes free while an innocent man takes his place. Whether Mark intends this theologically or simply narrates it as history is an open question, but the typological resonance is impossible to miss.

The cry of dereliction (15:34) — “My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?” — is a quotation of Psalm 22:1. This has generated enormous theological discussion. Three main interpretations: (1) Jesus is experiencing genuine God-forsakenness as he bears the sin of the world; (2) Jesus is citing the psalm as a whole, which ends in vindication and praise, so the cry includes implicit hope; (3) the cry represents the experiential reality of isolation without implying actual divine abandonment. Most serious interpreters hold some combination of (1) and (2). It is worth knowing that Psalm 22 does end in vindication — but that does not make the cry of dereliction any less real or painful.

The tearing of the temple curtain (15:38) is immediately juxtaposed with the centurion’s confession. The curtain separated the Holy of Holies from the rest of the temple — its tearing signals that the separation between God and humanity has been removed. The centurion’s declaration is the narrative’s climax. He is a Gentile, he has just overseen an execution, and he has no theological stake in who Jesus is — yet he sees clearly what Israel’s leaders have missed entirely.

Mark’s resurrection account (16:1–8) ends abruptly, with the women fleeing in fear. Most scholars believe the original Gospel ends at 16:8, with vv9–20 a later addition (they are absent from the best early manuscripts). The abrupt ending is almost certainly intentional: it forces the reader to decide what to do with what they have read. The women’s fear and silence is the last word — and the unspoken question is: what will you do?

keyPoints.body:
The cross looks like failure by every visible measure — and that is the point. Mark does not try to make it look otherwise. Help your group resist the impulse to move quickly past the darkness to the resurrection. The cross needs to be faced in its full weight before the resurrection can mean what it means. The centurion’s confession comes at the darkest moment, not after it has resolved.

The centurion’s declaration is the Gospel’s interpretive key. He is the first human voice in Mark to clearly identify Jesus as God’s Son — and he is the least likely candidate imaginable: a pagan soldier who has just executed an allegedly subversive peasant. His perception comes from watching how Jesus dies, not from knowing the scriptures or witnessing the miracles. This is Mark’s final comment on what it takes to see Jesus: not theological education or religious standing, but a willingness to look honestly at what is actually there.

The resurrection account ends with fear and silence, which is an unusual note on which to conclude. Press your group to sit with the openness of this ending. The angel’s message is clear — “he has been raised,“ “he is going ahead of you” — but the women’s response is flight and bewilderment. Mark is not tying things up neatly. He is leaving the question open: and you — what will you do?

pastorals.body:
This is the most emotionally significant chapter of the study. Come prepared for a wider range of responses than earlier sessions. Some people will find the cross moving in a way that produces genuine worship. Others will find it intellectually demanding (the cry of dereliction in particular). Others may be in personal seasons of suffering where the cross speaks directly to their experience. Create space for all of these.

The cry of dereliction (15:34) is often either over-explained or passed over too quickly. Resist both impulses. The most honest pastoral response is to acknowledge that this is one of the most theologically dense moments in the whole Bible, that it represents something real in Jesus’ experience that we cannot fully comprehend, and that it is the ground of the great claim that God in Christ has gone into the darkest place human beings can go — and come back. That is enough for a group setting.

For people who have experienced profound loss or suffering and felt God absent, the cry of dereliction can be extraordinarily meaningful — the realisation that Jesus himself cried those words, and that the Father did not immediately resolve his pain. Be attentive to this, and give it room if it surfaces.

The abrupt ending of Mark (16:8) can be unsatisfying for people who want resolution. Acknowledge that this is deliberate, and that the Gospel’s unresolved ending is an invitation rather than a failure of narrative craft. The story is not over. The question it leaves is not ”what happened next?” but ”what will you do next?”

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the discussion of the resurrection to become purely historical and apologetic — a debate about the evidence for an empty tomb. That is not the wrong conversation, but it is not the primary one Mark is inviting. The resurrection in Mark is not primarily an argument to be won; it is an event that demands a response. Keep the focus on what the empty tomb means and what it asks of us, rather than primarily on whether it happened.

background.body:
The two-stage healing of the blind man at Bethsaida (8:22–26) is unique to Mark. It is one of only two miracles in the Gospels that happens in stages, and its unusual character — why does Jesus not heal him fully the first time? — is almost certainly the reason Mark includes it where he does. It sits immediately before the Caesarea Philippi confession (8:27–30), functioning as a visual parable of the disciples’ condition: they can see something, but not yet clearly.

The placement is the key to the interpretation. Peter will immediately give the correct answer about Jesus’ identity (8:29) and immediately reveal that his understanding of what that identity means is seriously distorted (8:32). He sees — but only partially. The healing at Bethsaida is Mark’s way of preparing the reader to understand Peter’s confession in its proper light: a real but incomplete perception.

The use of saliva in the healing (8:23) appears in two other Gospel healings (Mark 7:33; John 9:6). It has no obvious theological significance and is probably simply a concrete physical action that accompanies Jesus’ touch — characteristic of Mark’s vivid, tactile detail. Do not over-read it.

The command not to go back into the village (8:26) fits Mark’s pattern of what scholars call the ”messianic secret” — Jesus repeatedly restrains those he heals from broadcasting what has happened, apparently to prevent a premature messianic movement that would misunderstand his mission. This is more pressing in the Jewish territory of Bethsaida than in Gentile regions.

As a closing chapter, this study invites reflection on the whole arc of the Gospel. Mark’s structure is bookended by two blind-man healings: the two-stage healing at Bethsaida (8:22–26) and the dramatic healing of Bartimaeus (10:46–52), which is immediate and complete. The movement from partial to clear sight is not just the story of one individual — it is the story of the disciples, and potentially the reader.

keyPoints.body:
The chapter has two movements: the two-stage healing as a picture of partial sight (8:22–24), and the second touch that produces clear sight (8:25–26). The key exegetical and pastoral point is that the movement from partial to clear sight is not achieved by the man’s effort — it is achieved by Jesus’ second touch. Clarity about Jesus is not the product of trying harder; it is the product of continued exposure to Jesus’ work in us.

As this is the final chapter, create space for genuine retrospection. The reflection questions invite the group to ask where their understanding of Jesus has changed during the study, and where it is still partial. This requires a degree of trust in the group that may not have existed in the first session — but if the study has gone well, it should be available now.

The image of ”seeing people like trees” (8:24) is a wonderfully concrete way to describe theological distortion. Encourage the group to use it as a diagnostic tool: where am I seeing something real about Jesus, but still getting the shape of it wrong? Where do I have a picture of Jesus that is more ”tree” than ”person”?

pastorals.body:
Some people in your group may be finishing the study with a deepened faith and real clarity. Others may feel that they have more questions than they started with — which is not a bad outcome, provided they are genuine questions. Still others may be in exactly the same place they were when they began. Be honest about all of these possibilities, and resist the pressure to manufacture a tidy conclusion.

The question ”where are you still seeing only partially?” requires honesty and some courage. Model it yourself: as the leader, share where your own picture of Jesus has been corrected or deepened by the study, and where you think you are still seeing partially. This gives the group permission to be honest rather than aspirational.

The final application challenge — making a commitment about how to continue growing in seeing Jesus clearly — is worth taking time over. Don’t let it become a vague aspiration. Encourage specificity: a particular Bible-reading plan, a particular person to study Scripture with, a particular group to join. Vague commitments rarely survive the first week. Concrete ones sometimes do.

End the session with prayer that is genuinely open — not the kind of prayer that assumes everyone has arrived at clear faith, but the kind that asks Jesus to continue his work in each person in the room, at whatever stage they are at. The two-stage healing is a model: Jesus is willing to keep working until the sight is clear.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the final session to become a comfortable celebration rather than a genuine reckoning. The point of the blind-man passage is not that clarity is inevitable for those who keep trying — it is that clarity comes through Jesus’ ongoing work, and we need to keep putting ourselves in the place where that work can happen. The session should end with people knowing what they are going to do next, not simply feeling good about what they have done so far.

paras: Rick Creighton grew up in Northern Ireland and studied maths at Oxford and then taught maths at a secondary school in England. After that, he studied theology at Moore Theological College in Sydney and London School of Theology.

He served as a church pastor in England for ten years, before moving to Nigeria in 2012, where he has been teaching at ECWA Theological Seminary Kagoro and Jos ECWA Theological Seminary. He has been involved with BeaconLight Training since 2003.
Rick is married to Alanna. They have three children: Asha, Conor, and Jack.

publisher.name: BeaconLight
paras: BeaconLight is a non-denominational Christian training organisation based in the UK, founded in 1995. Its aim is to equip Christians worldwide with practically-applied Biblical gospel ministry tools — resources designed to help people both equip themselves and equip others.

BeaconLight’s resources include CrossCheck, which enables Christians to explain their faith and lead others to Jesus, and Word@Work, a verse-by-verse applied devotional commentary of the whole New Testament. BeaconLight also provides printed Biblical resources to prisons, seafarers, police, and the armed services.
BeaconLight believes that Christian ministry should be free of charge, and all its online materials are available without registration or payment. The ministry is funded entirely by donations.
BeaconLight is a member of the Evangelical Alliance and Global Connections, and is a registered charity in England and Wales (Charity No. 1047046).
